title: Kitesurfing the Meltemi — Greek Islands Wind Guide
meta_title: Meltemi Wind Kitesurfing Greece 2026 | Conditions Guide
meta_description: Master the meltemi wind for kitesurfing in Greece. Learn conditions, gear, technique & safety. Find expert gear at Surf Store.
excerpt: The meltemi wind is Greece's legendary summer thermal, delivering consistent 15–25 knot conditions perfect for advanced kitesurfers.
body_html:
Understanding These Conditions

The Meltemi wind is Greece's defining summer phenomenon--a powerful, consistent north-to-northwesterly thermal breeze that funnels down through the Aegean Sea between June and September. Unlike Atlantic swell-driven winds, the Meltemi is a thermal wind: it builds as the sun heats the Greek mainland and draws cool air from the north. This means mornings are often light and choppy; conditions strengthen dramatically in the afternoon, typically peaking between 14:00 and 18:00.

If you're planning to ride the Meltemi wind kitesurfing in Greece, expect consistent 15-25 knot afternoon winds in summer, with gusts pushing 30+ knots on strong days. The water itself is warm (24-27°C) and glassy in the morning, becoming choppy and textured as the thermal effect intensifies. Critically, the Meltemi produces powered wind, not swell. You will not find meaningful waves on most Meltemi spots--the water is choppy from wind texture, not organised swell. This defines your board choice entirely.

The best Meltemi wind kitesurfing spots lie in the Cyclades (Antiparos, Tinos, Mykonos) and the Dodecanese (Rhodes, Karpathos), where the wind channels between islands and remains predictable. Spots like Tinos have become world-famous for their reliability and strong summer thermal cycles.

Best Gear for These Conditions

Because the Meltemi is pure powered wind with no swell, you need a twintip or freestyle board. Wave boards are unnecessary and will waste money and space in your quiver.

Kite sizes: Start with a 12-14m for afternoon Meltemi sessions (15-20 knots). A 15m or 17m is essential for lighter mornings or weaker days. If you chase big Meltemi (25+ knots), keep a 9-12m in your quiver for safety. We stock Duotone and Cabrinha kites across this full range, and they handle the Meltemi's gusty afternoon character extremely well.

Boards: Invest in a Cabrinha or Fanatic twintip in the 135-155cm range. This size balances freestyle capability, upwind performance, and speed in the Meltemi's choppy water. Lighter riders (under 70kg) should lean toward 135-145cm; heavier or less experienced riders go 145-155cm. Twintips are faster and more fun in thermal wind chop than wave boards, and you'll actually use them on 90% of Meltemi days.

Wetsuits: Summer Meltemi sessions mean warm water (24-27°C), so a 3/2mm or short-armed 2/2mm from ION or NeilPryde is all you need. Protect yourself from sun exposure and minor abrasion--many riders overheat in thick suits. A rash vest under your suit is often enough.

Helmet and impact shorts: The choppy water and gusty afternoon wind mean unexpected wipeouts. A certified kitesurfing helmet and padded impact shorts from ION are non-negotiable, especially for trick attempts.

Technique Tips

Meltemi riding demands discipline because the wind is so powerful and gusty. Start your day early if you're nervous--mornings offer lighter, more forgiving conditions. Wait until 14:00 or later if you want reliable power.

Anticipate gusts. The Meltemi surges and drops suddenly as thermals build. Stay centred over your board and use kite movements (figure-eights or loops) to manage sudden power spikes rather than leaning back hard. A quick kite stall or upwind move will save you in a violent gust.

Upwind ability is essential. Afternoon Meltemi often pushes you downwind fast; poor upwind technique means a long walk back. Use a low, aggressive edge angle and keep constant kite tension. Twintips excel at this--use them.

Water is choppy, not swell, so forget wave tricks. Focus on freestyle: transitions, raileys, grabs, spins. Choppy conditions actually suit tricks better than glassy water--your board bites more readily.

Accept that light mornings demand patience. If the Meltemi hasn't switched on by noon, go swimming or wait for tomorrow. Forcing it in 8-10 knots wastes energy.

Safety Checklist

  • Gust management: The Meltemi is notoriously gusty. Respect it. If you feel overpowered, depowering immediately by flying your kite, then land and drop down a kite size.
  • Offshore wind risk: Many Meltemi spots funnel wind perpendicular to the coast or slightly offshore. Know your spot's wind angle. If the wind pushes you out to sea, you must have strong upwind skills and/or a recovery plan (boat support, buddy system).
  • Crowded water: Popular Meltemi spots (Tinos, Antiparos) attract dozens of riders on peak days. Use safe launch and landing zones, maintain distance, and watch for kite collisions.
  • Sun exposure: 6+ hours of Mediterranean summer sun will burn you severely. Wear a rash vest, sunscreen, and sunglasses with UV protection.
  • Dehydration: Thermal wind riding is exhausting. Bring water, electrolyte drinks, and take breaks. Heat and wind conspire to dehydrate you fast.
  • Equipment failure: Gusts and chop are hard on kites and boards. Check your equipment daily, especially bridles, seams, and board edges. A broken kite far from shore is dangerous.
  • Know tides and currents: Island spots often have strong tidal flows. Understand local water movement to avoid being swept into rocks or other hazards.

Our Gear Recommendations at Surf Store

For serious Meltemi wind kitesurfing sessions, we recommend building a quiver of two kites and one solid twintip. We stock Duotone and Cabrinha kites in 12m, 14m, and 17m sizes--these brands handle gusty thermal wind exceptionally well, with predictable depower and responsive bar feedback. Pair a 12-14m with your most frequent wind strength, and add a 17m for lighter days or a 9-12m for strong gusts.

For boards, we carry Cabrinha and Fanatic twintips in the 135-155cm range. These boards are fast, forgiving in chop, and excellent for freestyle tricks. A single 145-150cm twintip will cover most riders across the full Meltemi season.

Wetsuits: Our ION and NeilPryde 3/2mm and 2/2mm range are perfect for warm summer Aegean water. Add a rash vest for comfort and sun protection. Finally, invest in a certified ION or Nobile helmet and impact shorts--wipeouts in choppy Meltemi water hurt, and protection pays dividends.

Visit surf-store.com to browse our full range of Duotone, Cabrinha, Fanatic, ION, and NeilPryde gear, all shipped across Europe with expert advice from our team. We've ridden the Meltemi ourselves and know exactly what works.

title: Kitesurfing the Mistral — South France Wind Guide 2026
meta_title: Mistral Wind Kitesurfing France 2026 | Surf Store
meta_description: Master the mistral wind for kitesurfing in France. Learn gear selection, technique tips, and safety. Find it at Surf Store.
excerpt: The mistral wind brings powerful, consistent conditions to Southern France. Learn how to harness this famous wind safely and effectively.
body_html:
Understanding These Conditions

The mistral wind is a powerful, cold northwesterly that funnels down France's Rhône Valley, creating one of Europe's most reliable and strongest thermal wind systems. Unlike coastal sea breezes, the mistral is a valley wind--it accelerates through the gap between the Alps and Massif Central, delivering consistent 12-25+ knot winds that can hold for days.

Mistral wind kitesurfing in France means riding flat, often glassy water. There is virtually no swell in the Rhône Valley; the wind is purely powered and thermal. Water state ranges from mirror-flat on calm days to choppy from wind chop alone. This is not a wave riding destination--it is a powered, freestyle and tricks playground. The steady, strong mistral wind makes it ideal for learning tricks, perfecting your freestyle, and building confidence in powered conditions.

The mistral typically blows hardest from November to March, though it can occur in spring and autumn. Morning sessions are often lighter; the wind builds through midday and peaks in early afternoon. Conditions are highly predictable: if the mistral is forecast, you can bank on strong, clean wind.

Best Gear for These Conditions

For mistral wind kitesurfing in France, board choice is critical. You need a twintip freestyle board, not a wave board. Twintips are designed for powered wind and tricks, with responsive edges, symmetrical shapes, and durable construction for flatwater freestyle.

We recommend a twintip board in the 135-150cm range for most adult riders. Lighter, more experienced freestyle riders may go smaller (130-140cm); heavier riders or those wanting more float and easier water starts should go larger (145-160cm). Brands like Duotone, Cabrinha, Nobile, and Point7 all produce excellent freestyle boards suited to mistral conditions.

Kite sizing depends on wind strength and your weight. In typical mistral wind (14-18 knots), a 12-15m kite is ideal for adults. Lighter riders may prefer 12-13m; heavier or less experienced riders benefit from 14-15m for easier control and more power. Duotone, Cabrinha, and NeilPryde all offer responsive freestyle and all-round kites that thrive in valley wind.

Water temperature in the Rhône Valley ranges from 5-8°C in winter to 12-16°C in spring and autumn. A 4/3mm or 5/4mm wetsuit is essential in winter; a 3/2mm works for milder months. ION and NeilPryde wetsuits offer excellent warmth and flexibility for extended freestyle sessions.

Technique Tips

Mistral wind is clean and steady, making it perfect for technique refinement. Keep your kite high and stable; the consistent wind means you don't need to hunt for gusts. Focus on edge pressure and board control rather than fighting the wind.

On flat water, your transitions and tricks are entirely dependent on kite work. Practice smooth, deliberate movements--every kite input is magnified when you're not using wave energy to help. Heel-side carves, edge transitions, and smooth kite loops are easier in mistral wind because the wind is so predictable.The valley funnelling effect means wind can shift direction slightly as afternoon heating changes. Stay aware of your location in the bay or lake; if wind direction drifts, relocate to stay perpendicular to the breeze.

Safety Checklist

  • Cold water: Even in spring, mistral areas are cold. Never underestimate hypothermia risk; always wear a 3/2mm minimum, bring a thick hood if winter riding, and limit session length in near-freezing conditions.
  • Strong, sudden gusts: The mistral can be powerful and gusty, especially near the valley sides. Keep your kite trimmed conservatively and be ready to depower quickly.
  • Flatwater rescue: There is no swell to help you recover if you're separated from your board. Always ride with a buddy, carry a safety leash, and practise your water start regularly. Pack a whistle and phone in a waterproof case.
  • Shore hazards: Rocky or shallow launch areas are common. Enter and exit in designated, clear zones. Check tide and water level before each session.
  • Weather changes: Although mistral is predictable, sudden pressure systems can intensify the wind dramatically. If conditions feel unsafe, exit immediately.

Our Gear Recommendations at Surf Store

For mistral wind kitesurfing in France, we stock a curated range of freestyle boards and kites proven in strong valley wind. We recommend a Duotone or Cabrinha twintip board in the 135-150cm range--both brands excel in flatwater freestyle and handle mistral's power brilliantly.

Pair your board with a 12-15m kite from Duotone, Cabrinha, or NeilPryde. These offer the stability and response you need in consistent wind. For wetsuits, ION 3/2mm and 4/3mm options keep you warm without restricting movement--essential for long, technical freestyle sessions.

Our team has real experience riding mistral conditions and can help you match board size to your weight, skill level, and style. Visit surf-store.com to browse our full range of twintip boards, kites, and wetsuits, or contact us for personalised advice on gearing up for the Rhône Valley.

title: Kitesurfing the Tramontane — Roussillon Wind Guide
meta_title: Tramontane Wind Kitesurfing France 2026 Guide
meta_description: Master the tramontane wind for kitesurfing in France. Learn conditions, gear choices, technique, and safety. Shop expert equipment at Surf Store.
excerpt: The tramontane is France's most powerful and consistent wind for kitesurfing, blowing cold and hard down the Rhône valley and across the Mediterranean coast.
body_html:
Understanding the Tramontane Wind

The tramontane wind is a strong, dry northwesterly wind that funnels down from the Pyrenees and across southern France towards the Mediterranean. It's one of Europe's most reliable and powerful thermal winds, driven by pressure differences between inland heat and cooler coastal air. If you're planning to go tramontane wind kitesurfing, you need to understand that this is a powered-wind scenario with minimal swell.

Unlike Atlantic swells that bring genuine waves, the tramontane delivers consistently strong wind across flat or choppy water. This is crucial: the tramontane wind kitesurfing experience is built on power, not swell. The water will be choppy--sometimes quite rough--but true wave faces are rare. This means your board and technique strategy must centre on freestyle, tricks, and powered riding, not wave manoeuvres.

The tramontane typically runs strongest from April to September, with peak conditions often arriving in the afternoon as thermal heating intensifies the pressure gradient. Wind speeds regularly reach 15-25 knots and can gust well beyond that, making it a serious wind for intermediate and advanced riders. It's relatively steady once it builds, which is excellent for progression and consistency.

Best Gear for Tramontane Conditions

When the tramontane is blowing, you need gear built for powered wind and freestyle, not wave riding. Start with your board: choose a twintip or freestyle board in the 135-150cm range from brands like Duotone, Cabrinha, or Fanatic. Twintips are perfect for the choppy water and allow you to ride switch (backwards), land tricks, and adjust your weight dynamically as the wind gusts and the chop shifts. Wave boards will slow you down and make the choppy conditions harder to manage; save them for genuine swell spots.

For kite size, the tramontane's strength means a 12-15m kite will suit most intermediate riders in typical 15-20 knot conditions. Lighter or smaller riders may prefer 12-14m; heavier or less experienced riders should start with 15m. Duotone and Cabrinha kites are excellent in these sizes and handle the variable gusts well. Always check the local forecast and wind strength before launching--the tramontane can shift from rideable to dangerous quickly.

Wetsuits matter too. Even in summer, the tramontane brings relatively cool air and splash from the chop. A 3/2mm or 4/3mm wetsuit from ION or NeilPryde will keep you comfortable during longer sessions. If it's early season (April-May) or late (September-October), consider a 5/4mm or hooded suit; the wind is cold and the water takes time to warm.

Technique Tips for Tramontane Wind Kitesurfing

The tramontane wind kitesurfing technique differs from wave riding because your goal is maintaining power and control in choppy water, not reading wave faces. Keep your weight centred and your kite movement deliberate--avoid aggressive stalling or diving, as the wind's gustiness can catch you out. The chop will throw your board around, so maintain a wider stance and use your legs as shock absorbers.Learn to read the gusts. The tramontane often arrives in pulses, especially when it first kicks in. Rather than fighting the chop, use it to generate pop for tricks or adjust your edge angle to stay upwind. Ride with loose ankles and flex your knees constantly; this lets you absorb the choppy water and maintain board feel. If you're learning freestyle or wake-style tricks, the choppy water adds challenge, but consistency of wind is your friend--you can practise the same move across multiple attempts without waiting for swell.

Stay aware of your downwind drift. The tramontane can blow you towards the coast or into hazards if your spot choice is poor. Always ride in designated kitesurfing zones and maintain clear sightlines to your launch point.

Safety Checklist for Tramontane Conditions

  • Wind strength monitoring: The tramontane can strengthen suddenly. Check wind alerts and have a clear abort plan if conditions exceed your skill level or local limits.
  • Chopping water hazards: Rough water increases fatigue and makes recovery more difficult. Wear a helmet and consider a buoyancy aid, especially if you're not an advanced swimmer.
  • Launch and land zones: Always use designated kitesurfing beaches. The tramontane can push you offshore or into shipping lanes if you're not careful.
  • Kite depower and durability: High wind means higher loads on your kite and bar. Inspect your equipment regularly and know how to stall your kite quickly if needed.
  • Hypothermia risk: Even in summer, extended exposure to cool wind and water spray can chill you. Warm up between sessions and don't ignore early signs of shivering or numbness.
  • Local rules: Many French coastal spots have specific opening hours and rider restrictions during tramontane season. Respect these--they exist for your safety and others'.

Our Gear Recommendations at Surf Store

For tramontane wind kitesurfing, we stock twintip boards from Duotone, Cabrinha, and Fanatic in the 135-150cm range--perfect for the powered conditions and choppy water you'll encounter. Pair your board with a 12-15m kite from Duotone or Cabrinha; both brands excel in variable, gusty wind. For your wetsuit, we carry 3/2mm and 4/3mm suits from ION and NeilPryde that balance warmth and mobility. All three combine to give you the responsiveness and durability needed for consistent tramontane sessions. Visit surf-store.com to explore our full range and get advice from our team--many of us have ridden in southern France and know these conditions firsthand.

title: Kitesurfing the Levante — Tarifa East Wind Guide
meta_title: Levante Wind Tarifa Kitesurfing Guide 2026
meta_description: Master the Levante at Tarifa with expert tips on wind patterns, gear selection, and technique. Shop kites and wetsuits at Surf Store.
excerpt: The Levante wind at Tarifa brings powerful, consistent easterly conditions beloved by kitesurfers seeking reliable performance and skill progression.
body_html:
Understanding These Conditions

The Levante wind at Tarifa is one of Europe's most reliable and powerful thermal/pressure-driven wind systems. Running from the east-northeast across the Strait of Gibraltar, the Levante is born from pressure differences between the Atlantic and Mediterranean, intensified by thermal heating of the Iberian Peninsula during spring and autumn.

Unlike some named Mediterranean winds, the Levante wind at Tarifa is fundamentally a powered wind system--not a wave wind. Whilst the Strait's geography can occasionally channel Atlantic swell, the Levante itself generates chop and texture rather than rideable wave structure. The wind typically ranges from 15-35 knots, building through the morning and often peaking in early afternoon before easing towards sunset. It's a thermal pattern: weak or absent at dawn, progressively strengthening as land heats up.

Expect gusty, choppy water conditions, especially on larger thermal days. Wind direction remains remarkably steady (east to east-northeast), making it predictable for planning sessions. Tarifa's famous for the Levante because it's so consistent--you can often count on it 4-5 days per week during peak season (March-May, September-November). Water temperature ranges from 14-16°C in winter to 18-22°C in summer, so thermal protection is non-negotiable year-round.

Best Gear for These Conditions

Because the Levante wind at Tarifa is a powered, choppy-water scenario with no significant swell, you'll want to focus on freestyle and all-around boards, not wave equipment.

Kite sizes: In moderate Levante (15-22 knots), a 14m or 15m kite is your bread and butter. When the wind builds to 25+ knots, drop to a 12m. Early morning light days (10-14 knots) may demand a 17m. We stock 12m, 14m, 15m, and 17m kites from Duotone and Cabrinha--both brands perform brilliantly in the Levante's gusty thermals, with responsive depower and stability across a wide wind range.

Board choice: Choose a Duotone or Cabrinha twintip board in the 135-150cm range. Twintips excel in choppy, powered conditions and let you progress tricks, jumps, and freestyle without fighting wave break-up. A Nobile or Point7 twintip is equally solid if you prefer those brands. Avoid wave boards here; the Levante doesn't deliver the swell quality to justify them, and you'll waste energy fighting chop instead of learning technique.

Wetsuits: Year-round, go for a 3/2mm or 4/3mm suit from ION or NeilPryde. Winter sessions (November-February) may push you towards 5/4mm or a hooded 4/3mm. The Strait's water stays cool even in summer, and the Levante often blows hardest in shoulder seasons when air temperature is mild but water hasn't warmed yet.

Technique Tips

The Levante's gusty character requires a light, responsive touch. Keep your kite movements smooth and anticipatory--don't jab the bar; instead, feather inputs as gusts arrive. The wind will lull and punch unpredictably, so maintain a wide, athletic stance on your board and keep your weight centred until you commit to a move.

In choppy water, absorb impact with bent knees rather than rigid legs. Flatten your board slightly through chop to maintain edge contact; lean back a fraction to keep your nose up. When jumping, sheet in hard and cleanly, pop early, and look ahead to land in a trough, not a peak.

The steady east-northeast direction of the Levante means you can work the same launch beach and landing zone reliably. Use this to your advantage--scout the wind window from shore, identify where gusts are strongest, and position yourself upwind of that zone before launching.

Safety Checklist

  • Thermal wind ramps: The Levante builds quickly mid-morning. Don't assume 8 a.m. conditions will match 11 a.m.--rig conservative and upgrade if needed. Better to walk back to swap kites than over-commit early.
  • Gust management: Wear a reliable waist-belt and use a safety leash in good condition. The Levante's gusts can catch you off guard, especially in light phases. A sudden gust can shift your weight unpredictably.
  • Cold water: Respect the 14-22°C water year-round. A 3/2mm minimum is non-negotiable; hypothermia risk rises fast in the Strait, and rescue response can be delayed in heavy sessions.
  • Crowded conditions: Tarifa attracts global talent, especially during Levante peaks. Keep a wide spacing from other riders, call your spot loudly, and watch for stray kites overhead.
  • Tidal flows: The Strait's tidal current is strong. Check tide windows before launching; outgoing (north-flowing) tides push you further from shore. Plan your session and position accordingly.
  • Equipment inspection: Inspect bridles, bladder condition, and bar lines before every session. The Levante's consistency can lull you into complacency, but gusty conditions punish worn gear hard.

Our Gear Recommendations at Surf Store

At Surf Store, we've equipped hundreds of riders for the Levante and know exactly what works. We stock Duotone and Cabrinha twintip boards in the 135-150cm range--these are our go-to recommendations for Tarifa's conditions. Pair your board with a 12m, 14m, 15m, or 17m kite from Duotone or Cabrinha, depending on wind forecast and your weight. Both brands offer exceptional gust response and depower, essential for thermal wind precision.

For wetsuits, we recommend 3/2mm or 4/3mm suits from ION or NeilPryde. ION's thermal linings excel in cool Strait water, whilst NeilPryde's mobility suits are ideal if you want maximum freedom for trick progression. If you're visiting in winter, upgrade to a 5/4mm or hooded 4/3mm--your shoulders and neck will thank you.

Don't overlook accessories: a quality bar and leash from your kite brand, reef booties (the beach is rocky), and a helmet for freestyle sessions. Visit surf-store.com to browse our full Duotone, Cabrinha, ION, and NeilPryde inventory, or contact our team--we've all ridden Tarifa and can advise you personally on load, style, and what's working right now.

title: Kitesurfing the Poniente — Tarifa West Wind Guide
meta_title: Poniente Wind Tarifa Kitesurfing Guide 2026
meta_description: Master the Poniente wind at Tarifa with our expert kitesurfing guide. Learn conditions, gear, and technique tips. Shop now at Surf Store.
excerpt: The Poniente wind brings consistent westerly conditions to Tarifa—here's how to read it, gear for it, and ride it safely.
body_html:
Understanding the Poniente Wind at Tarifa

The Poniente wind at Tarifa is a westerly thermal breeze that builds predictably throughout the day as the Strait of Gibraltar warms in summer and autumn. Unlike the Levante (easterly) wind that often delivers Atlantic swell, the Poniente is primarily a powered wind--steady but with minimal wave formation. You'll find consistent chop and wind-driven texture rather than organised swell lines.

This makes the Poniente ideal for freestyle, wave riding in the technical chop, and straight-line speed work. The wind typically starts light in the morning (0700-1000), builds steadily through midday, peaks in the afternoon (1300-1700), and can hold well into evening. You'll get 12-18 knots on average, with gusts to 20+ knots on stronger days. Water temperature ranges from 16°C in autumn to 18-20°C in summer, so a wetsuit is essential year-round.

The Poniente wind at Tarifa is most reliable from June through October, and it's a genuine thermally driven wind, not a valley wind like the Mistral. Because swell is minimal, your board choice should reflect that: twintip designs dominate, not wave boards. The chop is workable for tricks and aggressive manoeuvres, and the wind's consistency makes it perfect for building skills.

Best Gear for These Conditions

For the Poniente wind at Tarifa, you'll want a flexible, mid-sized twintip board that handles chop and allows freestyle progression. A Duotone or Cabrinha twintip board in the 135-150cm range suits most riders--they're responsive in choppy water and forgiving if you're learning tricks or hooked-in freestyle. For lighter afternoons under 12 knots, consider moving down to 130-140cm; for stronger days, 145-155cm gives you more float and power.

Kite sizing is crucial. A 14m or 15m kite from Duotone or Cabrinha works well for the Poniente's typical 12-16 knot range. If you expect gusts or stronger days, have a 12m ready; if light afternoons are common in your season, a 17m can keep you going. Avoid oversizing--the thermal nature of the Poniente means wind strength can vary quickly, and a kite that's too large will exhaust you in the chop.

Wetsuit choice matters. A 3/2mm suit from ION or NeilPryde is ideal for summer (June-August); for autumn (September-October), step up to a 4/3mm to stay warm. The water cools as the season progresses, and thermal winds can feel colder than their knot count suggests because of the maritime air mass. Booties or a wetsuit hood keep extremities protected in the Atlantic chill.

Technique Tips for Poniente Conditions

The choppy texture of the Poniente wind requires a slightly different approach than swell riding. Keep your weight centred and your knees bent--absorb the chop rather than fighting it. Use smaller, more frequent kite movements to maintain control; avoid big, sweeping movements that can catch gusts unpredictably.

For freestyle, the Poniente's consistency is a gift. Use the afternoon peak wind to practise tricks--your power is more predictable than in sea-breeze conditions. Position yourself parallel to the beach rather than exploring far offshore; thermal winds can shift or dip suddenly, and staying close to launch keeps you safe and in the sweet spot.

If you're new to the Poniente, spend your first session hooked in, getting a feel for the wind gradient and how the chop responds to your board pressure. Once comfortable, experiment with wave riding in the technical texture--it's more forgiving than it looks, and the consistent wind allows you to focus on technique rather than reading swell.

Safety Checklist for Poniente Conditions

  • Launch preparation: Check the morning forecast and wind strength by 0900. Thermal winds can accelerate rapidly; don't assume light morning wind will stay light.
  • Kite management: The Poniente can gust suddenly in the afternoon. Always have a smaller kite ready and know your limit. If you're consistently being dragged, downsize immediately.
  • Offshore hazard: Thermal winds can push offshore wind patterns into the Strait. Stay aware of your distance from shore and keep a reference point visible. Never ride alone.
  • Chop recovery: The wind-driven chop can disorient you if you fall. Practise your water-start in controlled conditions first, and stay calm if you lose your bearings.
  • Wetsuit fit: A loose wetsuit fills with cold water and saps heat quickly. Invest in a properly fitted suit, and rinse it in fresh water after each session.
  • Buddy system: Always ride with others. The Strait's maritime conditions can change fast, and a spotter is invaluable.

Our Gear Recommendations at Surf Store

For the Poniente wind at Tarifa, we stock Duotone and Cabrinha twintip boards in the 135-150cm range--both brands are proven in European thermal wind conditions and offer excellent durability in chop. Pair your board with a 14m or 15m kite from Duotone or Cabrinha; these sizes cover most Poniente conditions and are easy to manage when you're learning.

We also carry a full range of ION and NeilPryde wetsuits--3/2mm for summer sessions and 4/3mm for autumn protection. Add booties and a rashguard from ION, and you're ready for the season. Our team has years of experience in Tarifa and can help you dial in your setup for your skill level and the current forecast. Visit surf-store.com to browse our stock or contact us for personalised advice on the Poniente wind at Tarifa.

title: Kitesurfing the Bora — Croatia Adriatic Wind Guide
meta_title: Bora Wind Croatia Kitesurfing Guide 2026
meta_description: Master the Bora wind in Croatia with expert kitesurfing and windsurfing tips, gear recommendations, and safety advice. Shop now at Surf Store.
excerpt: The Bora wind transforms Croatia's coast into a world-class kitesurfing and windsurfing destination—learn conditions, gear, technique, and safety.
body_html:
Understanding the Bora Wind in Croatia

The Bora wind in Croatia is one of Europe's most reliable and powerful thermal winds, born when cold air from the Balkans rushes down toward the Adriatic coast. This northeasterly gale typically peaks in winter and spring, with consistent gusts ranging from 15 to 40+ knots. Unlike Atlantic swell destinations, the Bora produces powered wind conditions with minimal wave generation--the Adriatic's fetch and geography simply don't create meaningful swell even in strong conditions.

The Bora wind in Croatia arrives in distinct patterns. Morning sessions often start light as thermal energy builds; by midday, the wind strengthens steadily, reaching its peak in early afternoon before dropping slightly toward dusk. This predictability makes it ideal for planning your session. Water temperatures range from 8-12°C in winter to 18-22°C in summer, so appropriate wetsuit thickness is essential year-round.

Because the Bora delivers powered wind without significant swell, you're working with choppy, wind-driven water rather than organised wave faces. This means your board choice and technique must focus on staying powered, maintaining control in gusty conditions, and making the most of flat-water freestyle opportunities rather than wave riding.

Best Gear for Bora Wind Conditions

For the Bora wind in Croatia, your equipment strategy should centre on stability, control, and power efficiency in wind-driven water.

Kites and Sails: In light morning conditions (12-18 knots), choose 15-17m kites or 5.0-5.5m sails. Once the Bora strengthens mid-afternoon (20-30+ knots), drop to 12-14m kites or 4.0-4.5m sails. Duotone and Cabrinha kites handle the Bora's gusty character exceptionally well; their responsive handling lets you dump power instantly when wind spikes. For windsurfers, NeilPryde and Gaastra offer durable, forgiving sails built for thermal wind reliability.

Boards: Use a twintip board in the 135-150cm range for kitesurfing--think Duotone, Cabrinha, or Nobile freestyle/all-round boards. These designs excel in choppy, powered conditions and give you the stability and manoeuvrability to handle wind gusts without being knocked sideways. Windsurfers should opt for a JP Australia or Fanatic freestyle/wave board in the 95-110L range; these offer enough float to keep you powered yet responsive enough for control in variable wind. Do not use a directional wave board here--there is no meaningful swell, and wave boards are inefficient in flat-water power conditions.

Wetsuits: Winter and spring sessions demand a 5/4mm or 4/3mm suit from ION or NeilPryde; the Adriatic stays chilly, and the Bora often blows hardest when air temperature is coldest. Summer sessions (June-August) may allow a 3/2mm suit, but many locals stick with 4/3mm year-round for warmth and durability against wind chill.

Technique Tips for Bora Conditions

The Bora wind in Croatia tests your wind awareness and edge control.

  • Anticipate gusts: Watch the water surface for darker patches--these signal incoming wind surges. Move your weight to your heels and depower your kite or twist your sail off to shed sudden power.
  • Stay centred: Choppy water and gusty wind reward a low centre of gravity. Keep your stance wide and knees bent; this absorbs chop and lets you drive through without washing out.
  • Edge hard on the build: When the wind steadies and strengthens, carve upwind aggressively to build speed and stay powered. Use your board's edge to lock into the water and maintain control.
  • Practice powered transitions: Flat-water freestylers thrive here. Master your carving loops, handle passes, and powered slides--these manoeuvres demand precision in variable wind but are infinitely practisable on the Bora's consistent thermal cycles.
  • Manage your launch and landing: Morning light wind means a long, deliberate launch; afternoon gusts demand a quick, decisive one. Always scout the beach and have a solid support crew.

Safety Checklist for Bora Wind Conditions

  • Know the wind forecast: The Bora arrives suddenly and can sustain 40+ knots. Check NOAA, Windguru, or local Croatian weather daily. Cancel if gusts exceed your comfort or your equipment's rated range.
  • Buddy system: Never session alone. The Bora's gustiness and cold water demand a spotter on shore and a trained rescue partner in the water.
  • Dress for cold water: Hypothermia is a real risk in winter Bora conditions. Wear a 4/3mm or 5/4mm suit, gloves, and a hood if air temperature drops below 5°C.
  • Secure your gear: Wind gusts can flip unattended boards and blow away bags. Use sand anchors and keep your second kite or sail under a secure bag strap.
  • Check your harness and leash: Gusty conditions put extra strain on attachment points. Inspect your harness stitching, kite bridle, and board leash before every session.
  • Plan your exit: Choppy water and variable wind make it easy to drift downwind. Identify your exit zone, maintain a clear lane back to shore, and don't hesitate to call it early if fatigue or conditions worsen.

Our Gear Recommendations at Surf Store

At Surf Store, we specialise in equipping riders for exactly these conditions. For Bora wind in Croatia kitesurfing, we stock Duotone and Cabrinha twintip boards in the 135-150cm range, paired with 12-17m kites from Duotone or Cabrinha that handle Bora gusts beautifully. Match these with a 4/3mm or 5/4mm wetsuit from ION or NeilPryde, and you're set for consistent, powerful sessions.

Windsurfers benefit from our JP Australia and Fanatic freestyle boards in the 95-110L range, combined with NeilPryde or Gaastra sails in 4.0-5.5m sizes. We also carry backup harnesses, repair kits, and helmets--essentials when you're pushing your limits in Europe's most reliable thermal wind.

title: The Sirocco Wind — Mediterranean Kitesurfing Guide
meta_title: Sirocco Wind Mediterranean Kitesurfing Guide 2026
meta_description: Master sirocco wind conditions for Mediterranean kitesurfing. Learn gear selection, technique, and safety. Shop now at Surf Store.
excerpt: The sirocco wind brings warm, gusty conditions to Mediterranean waters—here's how to harness it safely and effectively.
body_html:
Understanding These Conditions

The sirocco wind is one of Europe's most distinctive and powerful weather systems. Born over the Sahara and travelling north across the Mediterranean, it arrives as a warm, sustained southeasterly gust that can transform a glassy morning into rippable conditions within hours. Unlike the mistral's valley-focused power or the meltemi's thermal consistency, the sirocco is a synoptic wind--driven by large pressure systems--and it can maintain strong speeds (20-35 knots) for days at a time.

What makes the sirocco unique for sirocco wind Mediterranean kitesurfing is its thermal character combined with its raw power. It brings Saharan dust and heat, which can reduce visibility and make the air feel thick. Most importantly for your session: the sirocco typically arrives with choppy, wind-driven water rather than significant swell. The Mediterranean basin is small and enclosed, so distant storms rarely generate the organised swells you'd find on Atlantic coasts. Instead, you're looking at short-period wind chop--fast, punchy, and often chaotic.

This is powered-wind kitesurfing territory, not a wave spot. Your focus is on using the wind's energy for freestyle tricks, speed runs, and technical manoeuvres rather than carving lined-up waves. The sirocco is relentless and forgiving in that sense: if you're skilled enough to handle strong wind, you can progress fast and train hard.

Best Gear for These Conditions

For sirocco wind Mediterranean kitesurfing, your board choice is straightforward: use a twintip freestyle board, not a wave board. The sirocco delivers power without swell, so you need a board that excels in flat water and wind-driven chop.

  • Board: A Duotone, Cabrinha, or Nobile twintip in the 135-150cm range is ideal. These boards are designed for freestyle and powered conditions. They give you the pop and control you need for tricks and transitions in chaotic water.
  • Kite size: In 20-25 knot sirocco winds, a 12m or 14m kite from Duotone, Cabrinha, or Point7 will keep you powered. In stronger gusts (25-35 knots), drop to a 12m or even a 10m. The sirocco's warmth and Saharan dust can affect air density slightly, so don't feel pressured to jump two sizes up if the wind feels strong.
  • Wetsuit: The sirocco brings warm air and water, especially in spring and early summer. A 3/2mm wetsuit from ION or NeilPryde is usually sufficient. In winter sirocco sessions (rare but possible), a 4/3mm will keep you comfortable during long sessions.

Technique Tips

The sirocco's chaotic chop demands respect for board control and edge awareness. Here's how to adapt your riding:

  • Stance and weight: Keep your weight centred over the board and your knees soft. The chop will throw you off balance if you're rigid. Flex constantly to absorb bumps.
  • Kite position: Work the kite aggressively. The sirocco's power is inconsistent--you'll hit lulls and surges. Keep the kite high (11 o'clock or 1 o'clock) and use bar pressure to maintain tension during flat spells.
  • Line awareness: Sirocco conditions often bring reduced visibility from dust and spray. Keep your lines clear of obstacles and be extra cautious near other riders. Flying a kite in a dust-laden wind can blur your depth perception.
  • Tricks and transitions: The chop can actually help your tricks if you're confident. Use the bumps as launch ramps for jumps and spins. Practice your slack-line manoeuvres in powered conditions so you're sharp when the wind is strong.

Safety Checklist

  • Visibility: Saharan dust can turn a bright morning grey. Check local visibility reports and consider wearing a rashguard with UV protection--the dust reflects intense sun.
  • Kite control: The sirocco isn't gusty in the traditional sense, but pressure systems can drive sudden shifts in wind direction. Keep your kite low and stable, and don't venture far downwind. Always have a clear beach run-off.
  • Dehydration: The warm sirocco wind is deceptive. You'll sweat inside your wetsuit and lose fluids faster than you realise. Drink water before and after your session, even if it doesn't feel hot out there.
  • Spot assessment: Not all sirocco spots are equal. Some locations (southern Spain, Sicily, Malta, Greece's southern coasts) experience stronger, more consistent sirocco conditions. Others see lighter thermal winds that mimic the sirocco's direction without its power. Scout your spot first or ask locals.
  • Weather windows: The sirocco is predictable if you track pressure systems. A strong low off the Atlantic will push the sirocco into the Mediterranean for days. Check a synoptic chart 48 hours ahead to prepare.

Our Gear Recommendations at Surf Store

At Surf Store, we specialise in equipping riders for European wind conditions, and the sirocco is one of our favourites to prepare for. We stock Duotone and Cabrinha twintip boards in the 135-150cm range, backed by kites from Duotone, Cabrinha, and Point7 in 10m, 12m, 14m, and 15m sizes. Pair these with a 3/2mm or 4/3mm wetsuit from ION or NeilPryde, and you're ready for whatever the sirocco throws at you.

If you're new to the sirocco or upgrading your quiver, contact our team at Surf Store. We have real on-water experience in Mediterranean wind and can help you dial in the right setup for your style and the specific sirocco conditions at your local spot.

title: Trade Wind Kitesurfing — Why It Is So Good
meta_title: Trade Winds Kitesurfing Canary Islands 2026 | Fuerteventu...
meta_description: Master trade winds kitesurfing in Fuerteventura. Learn conditions, gear sizes, technique tips & safety. Shop kites & boards at Surf Store.
excerpt: Trade winds deliver reliable, consistent Atlantic conditions for kitesurfing in Fuerteventura. Learn how to read them, choose the right gear, and ride safely.
body_html:
Understanding These Conditions

Trade winds are the reliable backbone of kitesurfing in the Canary Islands and Fuerteventura. Unlike thermal sea breezes or valley winds that build through the day, trade winds are persistent weather systems driven by global pressure patterns. In the Atlantic, they blow steadily from the northeast, typically arriving in spring and reaching peak consistency in summer and autumn.

What makes trade winds kitesurfing special is their predictability. You'll often find 12-20 knots with minimal fluctuation once they establish--ideal for progression and freestyle. The Canary Islands, positioned at the edge of the trade wind belt, benefit from this consistency whilst also receiving Atlantic swell in autumn and winter. Fuerteventura's exposed southern and eastern shores are particularly well-placed to catch both wind and swell.

However, trade winds don't guarantee waves. Swell is separate from wind. Autumn and winter swells arrive when Atlantic storms push energy toward the islands; summer trade winds can blow over flat water. This is crucial for board selection: if conditions are powered wind without significant swell, a freestyle twintip board is your tool, not a wave board. When autumn swell does arrive, the combination of wind and waves creates genuine wave-riding opportunities--then a wave board becomes relevant.

Best Gear for These Conditions

Trade wind conditions call for versatility in your quiver. Your primary setup should prioritise the powered-wind scenario, which dominates most of the year.

Kite sizes: Trade winds typically sit in the 12-17 knot range, sometimes reaching 20+ knots. A 12m kite is your workhorse for lighter days and freestyle sessions. A 15m kite handles the mid-range wind and gives you more power for tricks and jumps. In stronger wind (18+ knots), drop to 9m or 12m. Duotone and Cabrinha offer excellent stability in these conditions; NeilPryde is another trusted option.

Boards for powered wind: When trade winds blow over flat or choppy water, use a twintip board in the 135-150cm range. Duotone and Cabrinha twintips are industry standards--responsive, forgiving, and built for freestyle. This size suits most adults and handles both light and stronger wind without sluggishness. Nobile and Fanatic also produce quality twintips favoured by local riders.

Boards for autumn swell: If you're visiting during peak swell season (September-November) and significant Atlantic swells arrive, a wave board becomes useful. Look for a Cabrinha, Fanatic, or JP Australia wave board in the 145-160cm range. These are narrower and more responsive in turning than freestyle equipment. However, confirm actual swell forecasts before investing in a wave-specific board; many trade wind sessions remain flat.

Wetsuits: Year-round water temperature in the Canary Islands ranges from 18-23°C. A 3/2mm wetsuit works spring through autumn; upgrade to a 4/3mm or 5/3mm in winter months. ION and NeilPryde produce durable suits with excellent flexibility for kitesurfing. The Islands' subtropical climate means you'll rarely need heavy winter protection.

Technique Tips

Trade winds are stable but often variable in gust patterns. Master these adjustments:

  • Stay mobile: Consistent wind doesn't mean dead-flat power. Micro-gusts are common. Keep your weight centred and your kite active in the window to exploit sudden increases.
  • Freestyle advantage: Flat-water conditions are perfect for progression. Spend time on tricks--handle passes, wake-style jumps, and board control--since you're not fighting swell.
  • Read pressure changes: Trade winds can weaken in afternoon thermal heating or strengthen as cooler air moves in. Adjust your kite size and session timing accordingly.
  • Swell transitions: If swell arrives mid-session, switch your stance and weight distribution to match wave riding. Keep your edge clean and use the swell's energy rather than fighting it.

Safety Checklist

  • Offshore wind risk: Trade winds blow from the northeast; some Fuerteventura spots are exposed to offshore flow. Check wind direction relative to shore before launching. Offshore winds push you away from land--always have a clear exit strategy and consider a buddy system.
  • Thermal wind collage: Afternoon heating can create unpredictable swirls alongside trade winds. Morning sessions are more stable; afternoon sessions require careful kite control and awareness of shifting direction.
  • Swell impact on flat days: Even without visible waves, Atlantic swells create powerful currents. Respect rip tides and avoid getting pushed too far downwind.
  • Dehydration: Subtropical sun + consistent wind = rapid fluid loss. Hydrate heavily before and after sessions, and seek shade between runs.
  • Reef and rock hazards: Many Canary Islands spots have shallow reefs or rocky areas. Know your launch zone intimately and avoid downwind hazards.

Our Gear Recommendations at Surf Store

For reliable trade winds kitesurfing across the Canary Islands and Fuerteventura, we stock exactly what you need. We recommend a Duotone or Cabrinha twintip board in the 135-150cm range as your primary setup--these are proven freestyle performers that handle both light and strong trade wind days. Pair it with a 12m or 15m kite from Duotone, Cabrinha, or NeilPryde; this combination covers the full wind range you'll encounter.

For wetsuits, we carry 3/2mm and 4/3mm suits from ION and NeilPryde--both brands excel in subtropical conditions with excellent stretch and durability. If you're planning an autumn swell chase, talk to our team about a Cabrinha or Fanatic wave board in the 145-160cm range; we'll help you assess whether swell forecasts justify the investment.

All our kites, boards, and wetsuits are backed by our expert staff. We've ridden these conditions ourselves and can guide you toward the exact combination for your skill level and goals. Shop our Canary Islands-tested range at Surf Store today.

title: Sea Breeze & Thermal Wind — Kitesurfing Guide
meta_title: Thermal Wind Sea Breeze Kitesurfing Guide 2026
meta_description: Master thermal sea breeze conditions for kitesurfing. Learn wind patterns, ideal gear, and technique tips. Shop expert gear at Surf Store.
excerpt: Thermal sea breeze creates predictable afternoon wind for kitesurfing—perfect for progression and light-wind sessions when you know how to read it.
body_html:
Understanding These Conditions

A thermal sea breeze is a daily wind cycle driven by temperature differences between land and sea. As the sun heats the land during the morning and midday, warm air rises inland, creating a low-pressure zone. Cooler air over the sea is then pulled toward shore to replace it, generating a steady offshore-to-onshore breeze by afternoon. This thermal wind is a summer phenomenon, most common in Mediterranean regions and coastal areas with significant land mass.

Thermal sea breeze kitesurfing is fundamentally unpowered wind until thermal heating builds--usually between 14:00 and 18:00. The water remains relatively flat because this wind is driven by local heating, not distant swell systems. You will see chop and texture from thermal turbulence, but genuine wave faces are rare. The wind typically starts light (8-12 knots), builds to 12-18 knots by late afternoon, and can drop quickly after sunset. Consistency and strength depend heavily on season, latitude, and local geography.

For thermal sea breeze kitesurfing, expect variable gusts, wind shifts as the breeze organises, and a compressed window of rideable conditions. The advantage is predictability: if you know the thermal pattern, you can plan your session. The challenge is that morning wind is usually non-existent, and light-wind gear must be precise.

Best Gear for These Conditions

Thermal sea breeze conditions require light-wind kites and boards designed for underpowered riding. Because swell is minimal and the water stays flat, use a twintip freestyle board, not a wave board. Wave boards are built to anchor in swell lines and demand powered wave faces; they are too directional and heavy for flat-water thermal sessions.

For kites, choose a 12-15m Duotone, Cabrinha, or NeilPryde depending on your body weight and the season. Lighter riders (under 70 kg) may favour 13-15m; heavier riders should size up to 15m or larger. A larger kite catches light wind earlier and keeps you riding as the breeze softens toward sunset. Avoid smaller kites unless you are an expert light-wind rider with excellent board control.

For boards, select a Duotone, Cabrinha, or Fanatic twintip in the 140-160cm range for light-wind thermal sessions. Larger boards float easier in light wind and forgive weak edge pressure. If you weigh under 75 kg, 135-150cm works; heavier riders benefit from 150-160cm. Freestyle boards are ideal because thermal breeze is flat, and you can practise unhooked tricks or simple loops without fighting chop.

For wetsuits, expect water temperatures of 16-22°C (northern Mediterranean summer). A 3/2mm suit from ION or NeilPryde is standard; in early summer or cooler regions, a 4/3mm or 5/3mm provides extra warmth during long afternoon sessions. Choose spring suits or full suits with sealed seams to trap heat during unpowered first hour.

Technique Tips

In thermal sea breeze kitesurfing, water start reliability is critical because wind is light and inconsistent. Build confidence in shallow water with your kite at 12 o'clock (neutral), board edged perpendicular to wind, and smooth trigger pressure. Avoid aggressive movements; gentle back-hand pressure launches you forward.

Once riding, keep your kite moving continuously. Thermal wind is patchy, so let your kite hunt for wind pressure by using figure-eight motions in the power zone. Stay in control by riding unhooked; you cannot afford to lose your kite edge in light conditions. Ride with a relaxed stance, weight forward on the board, and allow your kite to do the work of propulsion rather than fighting for speed.Watch for wind lulls and organised gusts. As the breeze stabilises mid-afternoon, gusts become more predictable; ride the peaks and coast through flat spots. Anticipate sunset collapse by moving toward shore as the sun lowers, to minimise the distance home if wind dies suddenly.

Safety Checklist

  • Light-wind kite loss: Thermal breeze can die instantly. Always have a safety plan to drift toward shore; ride in a bay with a clear downwind safety zone. Never thermal-breeze kite offshore or in narrow channels.
  • Arm fatigue: Light-wind riding demands constant kite control and core engagement. Take breaks every 30 minutes; dehydration and fatigue are real risks during long afternoon sessions.
  • Sunset wind collapse: Plan to exit the water 30-45 minutes before sunset. Wind typically drops sharply as the thermal gradient weakens. Do not get stranded offshore.
  • Kite entanglement: Thermal gusts are unpredictable and can flip or invert your kite. Use a reputable trainer kite or large kite (12m+) that you can manage solo. Stay away from obstacles and spectators.
  • Water temperature: Summer thermal conditions encourage long sessions. Dress in proper wetsuit thickness; hypothermia can develop faster in light-wind endurance riding than in powered sessions.

Our Gear Recommendations at Surf Store

At Surf Store, we specialise in thermal sea breeze kitesurfing gear because our European customers experience these conditions daily through summer. We stock Duotone and Cabrinha twintip boards in the 135-160cm range, perfect for flat water and light-wind sessions. Pair your board with a 12-15m Duotone, Cabrinha, or NeilPryde kite tuned for light-wind lag and forgiving bar response. These brands excel in thermal conditions because their kites generate pressure in marginal breeze and recovery from stalls.

For wetsuits, we carry ION and NeilPryde 3/2mm and 4/3mm suits with sealed seams and thermal lining for long summer sessions. Match your kite and board choice with expert advice: our staff have years of on-water thermal breeze experience and can recommend exact setups for your weight, skill level, and local water temperature.

Stock is maintained year-round for summer conditions across Europe. Shop thermal breeze gear now at Surf Store and prepare for consistent, predictable afternoon wind.

title: Kitesurfing in 20 Knots — Kite Size & Setup Guide
meta_title: Kitesurfing 20 Knots: What Kite Size 2026
meta_description: 20 knots is ideal for progression. Learn kite sizes, techniques & safety for kitesurfing in 20 knots. Find gear at Surf Store.
excerpt: 20 knots offers perfect conditions for learning and progression—here's how to choose your kite size and master the wind.
body_html:
Understanding These Conditions

Twenty knots sits in that productive middle ground for kitesurfing. It's a wind speed where you have genuine power to generate lift, perform tricks, and launch jumps, but you're not fighting overwhelming gusts or struggling with overpowered equipment. At this velocity, water conditions typically remain choppy rather than swell-driven unless you're riding a dedicated wave spot with concurrent swell; in most powered-wind locations (thermal breeze zones, valley winds, or consistent trade wind destinations), 20 knots delivers predictable, freestyle-friendly conditions.

The key characteristic of kitesurfing in 20 knots is balance. You're neither underpowered (fighting to maintain speed) nor overpowered (wrestling control). Your kite has enough authority to keep you moving across choppy water, and your board responds decisively to weight shifts and edge pressure. This is why 20 knots is often considered the ideal learning wind speed--beginners can focus on technique without being thrown around, and intermediates can progress tricks and wave riding without constant equipment anxiety.

Best Gear for These Conditions

Kite Size: At 20 knots, a kite in the 12-15m range is your sweet spot. Heavier riders (80+ kg) should lean towards 15m or even 17m; lighter riders (under 70 kg) will be comfortable at 12-13m. We recommend Duotone or Cabrinha kites for their balance of stability and freestyle response. NeilPryde and Gaastra also deliver excellent performance in this wind. Your kite choice depends on your weight and the board type you're riding: freestyle boards need slightly larger kites to maintain air time and pop; wave boards need less kite size because water features provide additional turning leverage.

Board Type & Size: For 20 knots in most European locations--whether you're riding thermal winds, valley gusts, or consistent trade winds--a twintip freestyle board in the 135-150cm range is the default choice. Boards from Cabrinha, Fanatic, JP Australia, or Tabou excel here. Twintips allow you to ride switch, perform tricks, and adapt to choppy water without needing real swell. If you're riding a dedicated wave spot with confirmed swell (for example, Tarifa or Cabrinha Island with Levante/Poniente and Atlantic groundswell), a wave board from Fanatic or Cabrinha in the 145-160cm range becomes the choice--but standard European conditions (Mistral, Meltemi, thermal breezes) rarely produce the swell needed for wave boards.

Wetsuit: At 20 knots in summer or warm-water seasons, a 3/2mm wetsuit from ION or NeilPryde keeps you comfortable without overheating. In autumn, spring, or cooler Mediterranean regions, step up to 4/3mm. Winter sessions in Northern Europe demand 5/4mm or hooded suits. Wrist seals and booties reduce water flushing and extend comfort.

Technique Tips

At 20 knots, your kite sits in a narrow sweet spot in the wind window--you have power, but overstretching your reach will cause stalling or diving. Keep your kite between the two and ten o'clock positions (imagining the window as a clock face). Avoid lazy, wide figure-eights; instead, use smaller, sharper turns to maintain tension and pressure. This is ideal for learning edge control and building consistent edging: drive your heels and toes hard into the water, and let the board's rails do the work rather than muscling turns.

For jumping, 20 knots rewards timing over aggression. Pop your board at the peak of your kite's ascent rather than pulling hard; trust the wind to lift you. Land soft, absorb the impact with bent knees, and immediately steer your kite back to neutral to prepare for the next move. If you're riding a wave board in swell, treat your kite as a tool for speed generation and positioning: keep it stable and efficient rather than constantly looping or diving.

Safety Checklist

  • Kite Drift: At 20 knots, offshore wind can push you sideways. Always ride with a clear exit lane downwind; never position yourself near hazards (rocks, swimmers, shallow water).
  • Depower Control: Practise your depower technique. If the wind gusts to 22-24 knots, you should be able to dive your kite and slide the boom back smoothly. Know your bar's depower range and trim settings.
  • Foil Damage: Choppy water at 20 knots can bury your kite in whitecash--waterstart cleanly and avoid deep-water looping unless you're certain of depth and obstacles.
  • Leash and Release: Wear your leash and know your safety system. Test your quick-release before every session.
  • Other Water Users: Thermal breezes and valley winds often attract day-trippers and swimmers. Scout the water before launching, and maintain distance from pedestrians.

Our Gear Recommendations at Surf Store

For kitesurfing in 20 knots, we recommend a Duotone or Cabrinha twintip board in the 135-150cm range, paired with a 12-15m kite from Duotone, Cabrinha, or NeilPryde. Lighter riders should choose the smaller end; heavier or taller riders should move toward 15m or 17m kites. Add a 3/2mm or 4/3mm wetsuit from ION or NeilPryde depending on water temperature and season. If you're chasing waves at a dedicated swell location, we also stock Fanatic or Cabrinha wave boards in the 145-160cm range--but confirm swell and local conditions first. Our team has years of on-water experience at European spots; visit surf-store.com to browse our full range of twintip boards, freestyle kites, and wetsuits, or contact us for personalised advice on your weight, skill level, and local conditions.

title: Kitesurfing in 25–30 Knots — Strong Wind Setup Guide
meta_title: Kitesurfing 25–30 Knots: Guide for Strong Wind 2026
meta_description: Master strong-wind kitesurfing in 25–30 knots. Gear, technique, and safety tips from Surf Store experts. Shop now at Surf Store.
excerpt: 25–30 knots is prime strong-wind territory for experienced kiters. Learn the right gear, technique, and safety measures to ride these conditions confidently.
body_html:
Understanding These Conditions

Kitesurfing in 25-30 knots sits firmly in the strong wind category--the kind of conditions that separate beginners from intermediate riders. At this wind speed, you have genuine power on the water, but also increased risk if you're not prepared. Most European spots in this range--whether Mistral in the Rhône Valley, Meltemi in Greece, Bora in Croatia, or trade winds in the Canary Islands--are powered thermal or valley winds with little to no swell. This means your focus shifts away from wave riding and towards freestyle tricks, speed runs, and efficient power management. If you're kitesurfing in 25-30 knots at a location with confirmed swell (rare in Europe during strong wind), you might consider a wave setup; otherwise, stick with twintip boards designed for powered conditions.

The water state in these winds is usually choppy rather than swell-driven. Thermal and valley winds create rough texture because of the wind's sheer strength and direction changes, not because of incoming waves. Your board needs to be forgiving and responsive to chop, not optimised for carving down a face.

Best Gear for These Conditions

Kite Size: At 25-30 knots, most riders move down to a 12-15m kite. If you're heavier (85kg+) or prefer a more powered, stable feel, a 15m Duotone or Cabrinha gives you direct control. For lighter riders (under 75kg) or those seeking tricks and speed, a 12m kite offers better response and easier rotation. Many kitesurfers keep both sizes in their quiver for this wind range.

Board Type: In 25-30 knots without significant swell, use a twintip or freestyle board. Twintips are designed for these powered, choppy conditions and excel at tricks, jumps, and aggressive riding. We recommend a Duotone, Cabrinha, or Nobile twintip board in the 135-150cm range for most riders. Larger riders (90kg+) may prefer 150-160cm for stability; lighter riders (under 65kg) suit 130-140cm for manoeuvrability.

Wetsuit: Strong wind often means cooler air temperature or early/late season riding. A 3/2mm or 4/3mm wetsuit from ION or NeilPryde is standard for European waters in these conditions. If you're riding in summer or Mediterranean trade winds, 3/2mm is usually sufficient; winter or Atlantic-facing coasts may demand 4/3mm or thicker.

Technique Tips

Power Management: In 25-30 knots, your kite is always working hard. Keep your bar pressure light--avoid over-steering. Let the wind do the work; focus on fine adjustments and weight distribution across your feet rather than aggressive bar movements.

Stance and Balance: Stay centred on your twintip board. Strong wind amplifies small weight shifts. Keep your knees soft and your upper body relaxed to absorb chop. Lean slightly forward to prevent getting blown back; lean slightly aft only when launching into tricks.

Jumping and Tricks: At this wind speed, loops and jumps come easily if you're confident. Build height gradually--don't force big air on your first session. Use smooth, direct bar inputs; jerky movements lead to uncontrolled height or rotation. For freestyle tricks on flat water, focus on smooth transitions and controlled unhooked moves rather than massive airs.

Wind Shifts: Strong winds often shift direction or gust unpredictably, especially in valley or thermal conditions. Stay alert. If a gust hits, dump the kite skyward immediately; don't fight it. Position yourself in the centre of the window to have room to move if wind direction changes.

Safety Checklist

  • Helmet & Impact Vest: Non-negotiable at this wind speed. Falls are faster, and chop can disorient you. Wear a certified kitesurfing helmet and a padded impact vest.
  • Leash Check: Inspect your board leash before every session. High wind means higher velocity if you lose your board. A broken leash in 25-30 knots is dangerous.
  • Kite Check: Look for tears, leaks, and frame damage. A fatigued kite loses relaunch ability and predictability. Replace or repair immediately.
  • Launch Area: Choose a clear, wide beach or lagoon. Avoid crowded spots and stay upwind of other kites and swimmers. In strong wind, depower zones shrink and repositioning takes longer.
  • Know Your Exit: Identify where you'll go if you lose control. Have a clear downwind escape route away from obstacles, swimmers, and other riders.
  • Weather Window: Don't stay out if conditions worsen. If gusts push beyond 30 knots or wind becomes erratic, return to shore. Know your limits--strong wind amplifies mistakes.
  • Buddy System: Ride with a friend or in a zone where other competent riders are present. In strong wind, assistance matters.

Our Gear Recommendations at Surf Store

Kitesurfing in 25-30 knots demands reliable, responsive gear--and we stock exactly what you need. For strong wind twintip setups, we carry Duotone, Cabrinha, and Nobile boards in the 135-160cm range, all designed to handle choppy, powered conditions. title: Kitesurfing in 12–15 Knots — Light Wind Tips & Gear
meta_title: Kitesurfing Light Wind 12–15 Knots: Tips & Gear 2026
meta_description: Master kitesurfing light wind 12–15 knots with our expert guide on kite sizes, technique, and safety. Find gear at Surf Store.
excerpt: Learn how to kitesurfing light wind 12–15 knots conditions with the right kite size, technique adjustments, and safety practices.
body_html:
Understanding These Conditions

Twelve to fifteen knots sits in that challenging sweet spot--enough wind to fly a kite and move forward, but not enough to generate real power or rely on speed. In light wind, your success depends entirely on efficient technique, the right equipment, and patience. These conditions are common across European thermal spots (afternoon sea breezes, summer valley winds) and can occur anywhere as transition wind between stronger sessions.

At this wind range, you won't generate swell--the water will be choppy from thermal heating or local chop, not groundswell. This means twintip and freestyle boards are the correct choice, not wave boards. Your focus is staying upwind, maintaining momentum, and practising smooth movements rather than aggressive carving or wave riding.

Light wind kitesurfing in the 12-15 knot range rewards riders who understand body positioning, kite control, and edge pressure. It's frustrating if you approach it like powered wind; it's rewarding if you treat it as a technical masterclass.

Best Gear for These Conditions

Kite Size: In 12-15 knots, you'll need a 15m or 17m kite for reliable lift and control. A 12m kite is too small for consistent power at the lower end; a 17m gives you more margin if wind dips. Duotone and Cabrinha both make excellent light-wind kites with forgiving handling and good drift characteristics.

Board Choice: Use a twintip or freestyle board, 135-150cm in length. Twintips are your best option: they're light, responsive, and designed for powered riding without waves. Look for boards with good float (volume around 55-70 litres depending on your weight) so you stay upwind and plane easily. Brands like Cabrinha, Duotone, Nobile, and Fanatic all offer quality twintip boards in this size range that perform well in light wind.

Wetsuit: In temperate European waters (May-September), a 3/2mm or 4/3mm wetsuit from ION or NeilPryde is standard. Light wind sessions often last longer because you're working harder; staying warm keeps your technique sharp.

Bar & Lines: Use a quality bar with smooth, responsive control. Light wind demands precise kite steering; cheap bars with stiff lines will tire you quickly.

Technique Tips

  • Maximize Body Weight on the Board: In light wind, every gram matters. Keep your weight forward on the board, centred over the sweet spot. Back-foot pressure will stall you immediately.
  • Aggressive Upwind Riding: Angle your board 30-40 degrees off the wind direction and maintain constant edge pressure. Light wind demands more angle than powered conditions; don't point straight downwind.
  • Smooth Kite Movements: Large kite movements waste energy. Make small, deliberate adjustments to the kite's position--think millimetre tweaks, not radical swings.
  • Use Your Core: Light wind riding is exhausting because you're generating all the power through your body, not the wind. Keep your core engaged and distribute effort across both legs.
  • Avoid Tricks: Save freestyle for 15+ knots. In 12-15 knots, focus on staying upwind and building flow. Once you can hold a comfortable edge and ride smoothly, you've mastered the session.
  • Practice Transitions: Light wind is ideal for perfecting your switch and backward riding. The low speed makes falls harmless and recovery quick.

Safety Checklist

  • Wind Variations: Thermal wind is often gusty and prone to sudden drops. Always have an exit strategy and stay near the shore. If the wind falls below 12 knots, stop immediately rather than fighting to stay upwind.
  • Fatigue: Light wind riding is physically demanding. Your arms, core, and legs work harder. Take shorter sessions (45-60 minutes) and don't push into exhaustion--tired riders make poor decisions.
  • Depower Management: Use your kite's depower fully. In light wind, you may need to dump power to recover; ensure your bar depower system is set correctly before launching.
  • Spot Selection: Choose flat-water spots or protected bays. Chop and currents are magnified in light wind because you're moving slowly and have less control.
  • Buddy System: Never kite alone in light wind. If you get stuck or tired, a buddy can provide assistance or call the rescue service if needed.

title: Windsurfing in 20 Knots — Sail Size & Setup Guide
meta_title: Windsurfing 20 Knots Sail Size Setup 2026 | Surf Store
meta_description: Expert guide to windsurfing in 20 knots: ideal sail sizes, boards, and technique. Find your perfect setup at Surf Store.
excerpt: 20 knots is ideal windsurfing conditions. Learn the right sail size, board choice, and technique to make the most of moderate wind.
body_html:
Understanding These Conditions

Windsurfing in 20 knots represents a sweet spot for most riders: enough power to plane easily, but not so much that you're wrestling the board or kite across the water. At this wind speed, you'll find consistent, reliable conditions across most European spots--whether you're on a lake, bay, or coastal location.

20 knots is typically powered thermal or valley wind, not a wave-dependent scenario. Places like the Rhône Valley (Mistral), Greek islands during Meltemi winds, or Tarifa's summer thermals all deliver this kind of steady, directional breeze. The water will be choppy--chop comes from wind action, not swell--so you're looking at freestyle and freeride conditions rather than wave riding. If you happen to be at a true wave spot with real swell during a 20-knot day, you'll adapt your approach, but most windsurfing in 20 knots focuses on twintip boards and sails tuned for powered wind delivery.

This is beginner-to-intermediate friendly wind: easy to control, forgiving if you make mistakes, and consistent enough that you can focus on technique rather than fighting conditions.

Best Gear for These Conditions

Sail Size for Windsurfing in 20 Knots

At 20 knots, most adult riders will use a 12-15m sail depending on body weight and skill level. Lighter riders (under 70 kg) often go 12-13m; heavier riders (70-90 kg) prefer 14-15m. If you're on the larger side or new to the sport, start at the higher end of this range--extra sail area keeps you comfortable and gives you more control margin.

We stock sails from Duotone, Cabrinha, and NeilPryde in the 12-15m range. These brands offer excellent durability and predictable handling in moderate wind. A 13m is often the go-to all-rounder for windsurfing in 20 knots.

Board Choice

Use a twintip freestyle or freeride board, not a wave board. Twintips are bidirectional, lighter, and designed for the chop that powered wind creates. Wave boards need actual swell to justify their weight and wave-specific rocker; 20-knot thermal wind alone won't give you that. A twintip lets you jibe quickly, recover easily if you fall, and progress your freestyle tricks without fighting the board.

Board size: 135-150cm for most adult riders. Lighter or newer riders aim for 145-150cm (more flotation, easier to waterstart); heavier or more experienced riders go 135-145cm (more responsive, better for tricks). We carry Duotone and Cabrinha twintip boards in this range--both are industry-standard for European conditions.

Wetsuit Thickness

In summer or Mediterranean conditions, a 3/2mm suit is typical; in spring, autumn, or Northern Europe, move to 4/3mm or 5/4mm. ION and NeilPryde wetsuits are our stock favourites--they're designed for water sports and offer the stretch and durability you need for active windsurfing. Don't underestimate water temperature; cold water kills focus and safety.

Technique Tips

Stance and Balance

At 20 knots, the wind is predictable enough that you can focus on board position. Keep your feet roughly shoulder-width apart, weight evenly distributed. As you gain speed, move slightly forward to maintain plane; this reduces effort and improves control. If you feel overpowered, move slightly back to depower the sail.

Sail Trim

Play the boom height and angle. Too high, and you lose power and stability; too low, and you tire your arms. In a gust, ease the sail slightly or ease your grip on the boom. In a lull, sheet in a touch to maintain plane. This constant micro-adjustment is what makes windsurfing in 20 knots so rewarding--conditions are stable enough to refine technique, but varied enough to keep you engaged.

Jibing and Manoeuvres

20 knots is ideal for learning and practising jibes. The wind is strong enough that you'll plane through the manoeuvre but light enough that a mistake won't throw you hard. Commit to rotating your hips and shoulders early; let the board's twin-tip design do the work. Focus on smooth boom transitions rather than power.

Safety Checklist

  • Check your leash and harness: 20 knots is strong enough that losing your board becomes inconvenient. Always clip in, and inspect your leash for cracks or wear before each session.
  • Waterstart confidence: At this wind speed, you'll be up and riding quickly, but if you fall (and you will), your waterstart needs to be solid. Practise on shallower water first if you're new to the spot.
  • Watch for gusts: Thermal wind often arrives in pulses. If a gust hits and you're not ready, ease the sail or rotate slightly to spill wind. Don't panic; gusts at 20 knots are manageable if you stay loose.
  • Monitor other riders and hazards: Chop makes visibility tricky. Know where other sailors are, and maintain clear space for your jibes.
  • Exit strategy: Always know where to go if conditions get tough. A shallow beach or calm bay is your friend on a windy day.

Our Gear Recommendations at Surf Store

For windsurfing in 20 knots, we recommend pairing a Duotone or Cabrinha twintip board in the 135-150cm range with a 13-15m sail from Duotone, Cabrinha, or NeilPryde. These combinations are proven across European conditions--stable enough for newcomers, responsive enough for experienced riders.

Add a 3/2mm or 4/3mm wetsuit from ION or NeilPryde (depending on season and location), a quality harness, and a reliable leash. We ship across Europe and stock everything you need for confident, safe windsurfing sessions.
