title: SUP Paddling in Wind — How to Handle Windy Conditions
meta_title: SUP Paddling in Wind: How to Handle Windy Conditions
meta_description: Master SUP paddling in wind with expert techniques, stance tips, and gear advice. Learn how to handle windy conditions safely. Find it at Surf Store.
excerpt: Control your SUP in wind by adopting a wider stance, paddling with shorter strokes, angling your board into gusts, and choosing a narrower board with good di...
body_html:
Windy conditions on a SUP require a lower centre of gravity, a wider stance, and deliberate paddle technique to stay stable and moving forward. The key is understanding how wind affects your board's drift and learning to brace and edge your board effectively. With the right approach, you can paddle safely and enjoyably even when the wind picks up.

The Full Answer

SUP paddling in wind is fundamentally about balance and control. Wind doesn't just push you sideways--it catches your body and board, making you unstable if you're standing upright. Your board becomes a sail, and you need to reduce its effective surface area while maximising your stability.

The core principle is lowering your centre of gravity. A bent knee, crouched stance keeps your weight low and your board responsive. Your paddle also matters: shorter, quicker strokes are far more effective than long reaches that lift you higher and catch more wind.

Board choice affects how you experience wind too. A wider, thicker board with good volume gives you more stability platform, which is why many paddlers switch to a larger board when conditions get choppy and gusty. Wind also creates chop and uneven water, so you'll need active edge control and weight distribution to stay upright.

Practical Guide

Step 1: Adopt the Correct Stance

Stand with your feet slightly wider than shoulder-width and keep your knees bent at around 15-30 degrees. Your weight should be centred over the board's midline, not at the tail or nose. This lower, wider base is your primary defence against wind-induced instability.

Step 2: Adjust Your Paddle Technique

In wind, use shorter, more frequent strokes rather than long reaches. Keep the blade close to your body and your upper hand lower than usual. This reduces the amount of your body exposed to wind gusts and keeps you more balanced. Focus on smooth, continuous paddling rather than powerful, extended strokes.

Step 3: Use Edge Control

Subtle edging--tilting the board's rail slightly into the water--helps you track into the wind and resist sideways drift. Tilt the rail on your downwind side to create a slight edge that holds your line. Don't overdo it; a small edge is more effective than a dramatic lean.

Step 4: Angle Your Approach

Rather than paddling straight into strong wind, angle slightly to one side. This reduces wind resistance and creates forward momentum more easily. You can zigzag upwind rather than fighting a direct headwind.

Step 5: Choose the Right Conditions and Gear

On very windy days, opt for a board with higher volume and width. A 10'6"-11'6" all-round board with good thickness is far more forgiving than a 9'0" narrow touring board. Wider boards provide a more stable platform when SUP paddling in wind and chop.

Common Mistakes

Standing Too Upright - Beginners often maintain their normal stance, which raises their centre of gravity and makes them vulnerable to gusts. Bend your knees and stay low from the start.

Using a Board That's Too Small - A narrow 9'0" is stylish but unstable in wind. If you're learning or paddling regularly in variable conditions, more volume and width are your friends.

Overextending Your Paddle Stroke - Long reaches feel powerful but they lift you up and catch wind. Shorter strokes keep you stable and actually more efficient in choppy, gusty conditions.

Ignoring Edge Control - Many paddlers paddle flat, which means the board's edge cannot resist drift. Learn to feel the subtle pressure needed to hold an edge, especially when heading into wind.

Paddling Alone in Severe Wind - Always check conditions and tell someone where you're going. If gusts are above 25 knots and the chop is substantial, it's better to train on flat water and build your skills there first.

Surf Store Recommendation

For reliable SUP paddling in wind and variable conditions, we stock all-round and touring boards from JP Australia, Fanatic, and Nobile. These brands specialise in durable, stable designs with excellent volume distribution across a range of sizes--typically 9'6" to 11'6"--that handle wind and chop well without sacrificing performance.

Pair your board with a quality paddle designed for SUP (not canoe paddles) and always wear a properly fitted personal flotation device or leash attachment system. If you're unsure which board volume or size suits your weight and skill level in windy conditions, our team at Surf Store have real on-water experience and can guide you to the right choice. Visit us to discuss your conditions and paddling goals, or browse our SUP range online and get in touch with any questions.

title: Kitesurfing in Shallow Water — Safety & Technique Guide
meta_title: Kitesurfing Shallow Water Safety Tips 2026
meta_description: Learn essential kitesurfing shallow water tips, reef awareness, and safety techniques. Find expert gear at Surf Store.
excerpt: Shallow water kitesurfing requires smaller gear, precise technique, and constant awareness of the seabed to avoid impact injuries and equipment damage.
body_html:
Kitesurfing shallow water safely comes down to three essentials: using a smaller, more responsive board, understanding your exact water depth and obstacles before you launch, and maintaining absolute control of your kite at all times. Shallow water--roughly ankle to knee depth--demands a different mindset than open-water riding, but it's absolutely doable once you respect the risks and prepare properly.

The Full Answer

Shallow water kitesurfing is appealing because you can ride closer to shore, stay in view of support, and practise tricks without deep-water consequences. However, the seabed and hidden rocks create serious hazards. When you fall or lose control in shallow water, your board, line, or body can hit the ground hard. Reefs, rocky patches, and sudden depth changes multiply that danger.

Safety in shallow water depends on three overlapping factors: equipment choice, environmental awareness, and technique. You'll need a board that responds instantly to tiny movements--typically 135-155 cm for most riders--so you can avoid obstacles and keep moving. You must scout the area at low tide, identify rocks or reef edges, and mark mental (or actual) safe zones. Finally, your kite control must be sharp enough that you can dump power instantly, abort a manoeuvre, or land safely if something goes wrong.

The golden rule: shallow water kitesurfing is not for beginners learning to launch and land. Master those skills in deep water first. Once you're confident in your kite and board control, shallow water becomes a rewarding addition to your sessions.

Practical Guide

Before You Launch:

  • Visit the spot at low tide and walk the entire shallow zone. Mark rocks, reefs, channels, and sudden drop-offs mentally or with buoys.
  • Check local tide tables and wind forecasts. Shallow water kitesurfing is safest on rising tide, when water depth increases, and in steady, moderate wind (12-16 knots for most).
  • Brief a partner or observer on the plan. They should watch for kite angles, tangled lines, and sudden wind shifts.
  • Wear impact protection: helmet, knee pads, and elbow pads. Shallow ground hits are sudden and hard.

During the Session:

  • Keep your kite high (11-12 o'clock position) most of the time. This maintains power without committing to aggressive manoeuvres in tight spaces.
  • Ride within a pre-defined corridor. Know your exit route--deeper water or a clear beach--before you begin.
  • Avoid tricks that require big air, long flights, or loss of board contact (handle passes, wake-style jumps). Stick to carving, speed control, and basic freestyle tricks with quick board recovery.
  • If you sense instability--gusty wind, kite drifting, board hitting something--immediately angle your board downwind and steer away. Do not fight the conditions.

Exiting Safely:

  • Plan your exit before you need it. Know which direction leads to deeper water or beach.
  • If you fall or lose control, swim toward shallow beach immediately. Retrieve your board and kite calmly; do not panic or thrash.
  • Always carry a knife or quick-release tool to cut lines if you get tangled or held underwater.

Common Mistakes

Underestimating depth changes: A spot may feel safe at one tide but turn treacherous at another. Always check tide timing and water depth before launching.

Flying the kite too low: Beginners often dump the kite into the water or toward shallow ground. Keep it high to maintain control and prevent crashes.

Attempting big tricks too early: Jumps, spins, and handle passes require room to land safely. Shallow water kitesurfing demands humility--master basics in deep water first.

Ignoring wind shifts: Shallow water can channel or accelerate wind unpredictably. Stay alert and ready to dump power or exit quickly.

Riding alone: Always have a buddy or observer. If something goes wrong--entanglement, injury, sudden illness--you need help nearby.

Surf Store Recommendation

For shallow water kitesurfing, you'll benefit from a smaller, punchy freestyle or wave board. We stock freestyle and wave boards from Duotone, Cabrinha, Nobile, and Tabou in the 135-155 cm range--sizes that respond instantly to edge pressure and line input, keeping you in control over uneven ground.

Pair that with a durable harness from ION or Duotone (so you're comfortable during longer, careful sessions) and a quality bar and line set from Duotone, NeilPryde, or Cabrinha. For shallow water, reliability matters more than cutting-edge performance.

Our team has real shallow-water experience across European spots--from Baltic lagoons to Mediterranean bays. We can recommend the right board size and type for your weight, skill level, and local conditions. Visit Surf Store or contact us for personalised guidance on shallow water kitesurfing gear.

title: Kitesurfing with Tidal Current — Safety & Planning Guide
meta_title: Kitesurfing Tidal Current Safety Tips 2026
meta_description: Learn how tidal currents affect kitesurfing performance and safety. Expert tips to master wind and water conditions. Shop at Surf Store.
excerpt: Tidal currents create variable water flow that affects your drift, launch difficulty, and safety—understanding them is essential for confident riding.
body_html:
Tidal currents are invisible rivers of water that flow in and out with the moon's rhythm. They push you sideways or out to sea, even when the wind is perfect, and they directly affect how much energy you need to stay safe, how far you'll drift, and whether you can get back to shore easily. Ignoring them is one of the biggest reasons experienced riders end up in trouble.

The Full Answer

Tidal currents flow parallel to the shore (alongshore current) or perpendicular to it (rip current). In most European spots--the Wadden Sea, Tarifa, Pozo Izquierdo, Cabarete--the tidal push is so strong that it becomes the dominant factor in your session planning, sometimes more important than wind strength itself.

Here's what happens: if you're riding on a slack or incoming tide, the water beneath you is stationary or moving toward shore. Your ground speed matches your actual kite speed. But during an outgoing (ebb) tide, especially in a narrow channel or inlet, the current can push you seaward at 1-3 knots even if you're standing still on your board. Over a 2-hour session, that's a kilometre drift you didn't intend.

The effect compounds when you're learning. Beginners use more energy to stay upwind, and if a strong outgoing current is pushing you away, you'll exhaust yourself fighting an invisible force. Experienced riders know to launch and land perpendicular to the current, position themselves upcurrent of their exit point, and use the ebb tide as a natural highway if they're confident in their skills.

Practical Guide

Check the tide before you go. Use a free tidal prediction app or your local spot's tide tables. Note the timing and strength. In strong-current zones (Pozo, Tarifa), the difference between slack and peak ebb can be 2+ knots. That's worth a 2-hour wait if conditions are marginal.

Plan your entry and exit. Always position yourself so you can walk or swim back to shore against the current if you lose your kite. If the current flows parallel to shore, launch and land at the same spot. If it flows out, launch at a point slightly downwind of where you want to exit, so the current naturally brings you back.

Understand the three phases:

  • Slack tide (30-60 minutes around high or low water): Current is weakest. Best time for beginners or if conditions are borderline. You'll stay put relative to the water.
  • Rising/falling (2-4 hours mid-tide): Current is accelerating. Expect noticeable drift. Plan for extra time or energy if heading upwind.
  • Peak ebb/flood (strongest current): Only for confident riders in flat-water or downwind conditions. Outgoing tide can be dangerous if you're learning.

Use current as a tool, not a trap. Experienced riders use outgoing tide to extend their session range--they ride downwind knowing the current is taking them out, then let it carry them back on the way in. But this requires solid self-rescue skills and knowledge of your spot's geography.

Common Mistakes

Not checking tide before you go. Many beginners assume wind is the only variable. They arrive at a spot with good wind but foul tide, get pushed out faster than expected, panic, and end up in an exhausting rescue situation.

Fighting the current instead of using it. If the tide is pushing you downwind, and you're already tired, trying to fight back upwind will finish you. Better to ride downwind in the direction of the current and reassess.

Underestimating the impact on energy. A 1.5-knot outgoing current doesn't sound like much, but over 90 minutes it's genuinely fatiguing. Beginners should only kitesurf slack tide or incoming tide until they're fully confident in deep-water self-rescue.

Launching in the strongest current. The current is fastest at the point of exit, right where you're trying to get your kite in the air. Launch slightly upwind of this zone if possible.

Surf Store Recommendation

Safe kitesurfing in tidal current depends on gear that gives you control and confidence. We stock freestyle and wave boards from Duotone, Cabrinha, and Nobile in sizes 135-155cm, which offer the manoeuvrability you need to position yourself correctly against drift. Pair them with ION or NeilPryde harnesses designed for comfort on longer sessions--because fighting current takes stamina.

For flat-water spots prone to strong outgoing tides, consider a board in the 135-145cm range; they're easier to manoeuvre upwind if you need to work against the drift. Our team at Surf Store have real experience reading tidal conditions at European spots, and we're happy to advise you on the best gear for the spot you're planning to visit. Get in touch or visit us online to discuss your local conditions and find the right setup.

title: Lake vs Ocean Windsurfing — Key Differences & What to Expect
meta_title: Lake vs Ocean Windsurfing 2026 | Conditions Guide
meta_description: Understand windsurfing lake vs ocean conditions differences. Learn which suits your level. Shop boards and gear at Surf Store.
excerpt: Lake windsurfing offers flat water and consistent wind, while ocean windsurfing presents waves, currents, and more demanding conditions.
body_html:
Lake and ocean windsurfing are fundamentally different experiences. Lakes offer flat water, predictable wind, and calmer conditions ideal for learning technique, whilst oceans present waves, variable wind, salt spray, and currents that demand more advanced skills and resilient equipment.

The Full Answer

The difference between lake and ocean windsurfing conditions shapes everything--from the board you choose to the sails you rig and the techniques you use. Understanding these differences is essential whether you're a beginner selecting your first spot or an intermediate sailor expanding your range.

Lake windsurfing conditions are typically flat or nearly flat, with wind that builds gradually and remains relatively steady throughout the day. Water temperature is often warmer in summer months, allowing lighter wetsuits or rash guards. Lakes are also shallower, which means shorter swims to the beach if you fall. The psychological advantage is huge for learning: fewer variables mean you can focus on footwork, sail handling, and board control without worrying about wave impact.

Ocean windsurfing conditions introduce waves (ranging from small rollers to serious swells), stronger and more variable wind patterns, salt water that corrodes equipment faster, and ocean currents that can push you off course. Water temperature is often colder, requiring thicker wetsuits year-round in European waters. The unpredictability demands quicker decision-making and more robust technique, but the reward is access to some of Europe's most exciting windsurfing spots.

Practical Guide

If you're choosing between lake and ocean windsurfing, follow these guidelines:

  • Starting out: Begin on a lake. Flat water lets you master the basics--getting up on the board, steering, and sail trim--without the distraction of waves. This accelerates your learning curve by 3-6 months compared to ocean learning.
  • Moving to ocean windsurfing: Transition when you can confidently uphaul and control your board in steady wind. Start with small, mellow days (under 2ft swells). Learn to read wave patterns and position yourself for smooth water channels between sets.
  • Wind windows: Lakes often have lighter, steadier wind; oceans have stronger but choppier gusts. In ocean conditions, rig a sail 0.5-1.0 m² smaller than you'd use on flat water to maintain control in gusty conditions.
  • Seasonal strategy: Lake windsurfing peaks in spring and autumn when wind is strongest. Ocean windsurfing is most accessible in winter and early spring, when Atlantic swells are consistent and water temperatures are coldest (bringing better wind patterns).

Common Mistakes

Beginners often underestimate how different ocean windsurfing conditions are from lake experience. Here's what to avoid:

  • Rigging too large: Many lake sailors rig the same sail size for their first ocean session and get overpowered by gusty, choppy wind. Downsize your sail by 1-2 m² when you start ocean windsurfing.
  • Ignoring currents: Lakes have no currents; oceans do. You can drift miles off your intended route without noticing. Always know the tide and set a landmark as your reference point.
  • Wrong board choice: A lake freestyle board (small, light, thin rails) will be destroyed by ocean chop and salt spray. Ocean windsurfing demands a more robust wave or freeride board with stronger construction.
  • Neglecting equipment care: Salt water corrodes metal fittings, booms, and mast tracks rapidly. Rinse all gear in fresh water within 24 hours, or you'll shorten its lifespan by years.
  • Underestimating recovery difficulty: In lakes, a short swim gets you back to shore. In oceans, currents and distance make rescue harder. Always wear a leash and tell someone where you're windsurfing.

Surf Store Recommendation

For lake windsurfing, choose a freeride board (90-130 litres for adults) from brands like Fanatic or JP Australia--they're responsive, forgiving, and easy to control in flat water. Pair it with a sail from Duotone, NeilPryde, or Cabrinha in the 5.0-6.0 m² range for beginner progression.

For ocean windsurfing conditions, we recommend wave boards (70-95 litres) or freeride boards with thicker rails from Nobile or Fanatic. These handle chop and swells without breaking. Choose durable wave sails from Gaastra or Point7 that are built for gusty, variable wind. You'll also want a thicker wetsuit (5/4mm or 6/5mm) suitable for year-round European water temperatures.

Visit surf-store.com to explore our full range of windsurfing boards and sails matched to lake or ocean conditions. Our staff has years of on-water experience and can guide you to the right setup for your chosen environment.

title: When Should You Switch to a Smaller Kite? — Wind Guide
meta_title: When to Switch to Smaller Kite | Overpowered Guide 2026
meta_description: Learn when you're overpowered and need a smaller kite. Signs, conditions, and expert tips. Find it at Surf Store.
excerpt: Switch to a smaller kite when you feel overpowered, lose board control, or struggle to stay upwind.
body_html:
You should switch to a smaller kite when you feel overpowered: your board is harder to control, you're being pulled upwind uncontrollably, or you're fighting excessive bar pressure. These are clear signs the wind has increased beyond what your current kite size can safely deliver. Recognising this moment and acting on it will improve your safety, confidence, and progression on the water.

The Full Answer

Wind conditions change throughout the session, and what felt perfect at 12 knots may become dangerous at 16 knots. A smaller kite reduces power, making it easier to manage in strong wind and keeping you in control of your board and body. When to switch to smaller kite overpowered situations come down to how the kite feels in your hands and how your board responds beneath your feet.

The most common signs you need a smaller kite include:

  • Your bar is pulled hard toward your body, even with full depower
  • You're being dragged upwind and can't steer downwind easily
  • Your board is difficult to control, especially during transitions or turns
  • Your arms and shoulders are fatigued from fighting the bar pressure
  • You're unable to land tricks or maintain smooth edges

Going smaller isn't admitting defeat--it's respecting the conditions and maximising your time on the water. A properly sized kite for the current wind gives you more finesse, better tricks, and genuine enjoyment rather than white-knuckle survival mode.

Practical Guide

Step 1: Assess Your Feel
Before switching, ask yourself honestly: am I struggling, or am I just working hard? Light fatigue and focus are normal. Feeling completely out of control or unable to depower the kite further means it's time to change.

Step 2: Check Wind Direction and Strength
Look for wind shadows, chop patterns, and other riders' kite sizes. If everyone else is on smaller kites, you should follow. Wind often patches stronger in certain zones--move to a lighter area first to confirm the wind has actually strengthened.

Step 3: Swap Down Methodically
If you're currently on a 17m, go to 14m or 15m, not straight to 12m. A one-size drop is usually enough. Ride for 5-10 minutes to feel the difference before deciding whether you need to go even smaller.

Step 4: Use Your Depower First
Before switching kites, move your bar fully back. If you still feel overpowered with maximum depower and your kite is relatively flat, then it's genuinely time to go smaller. If you're still at 80% bar extension, you have depower margin left.

Common Mistakes

Waiting Too Long. Many riders tough it out for an extra hour rather than swap. This leads to poor technique, fatigue, and safety risks. The wind won't drop--swap early and enjoy better progression.

Swapping Too Far Down. Jumping from 17m to 12m is excessive unless wind has doubled. You'll end up underscreened and spend the session relaunching. Trust smaller increments.

Ignoring Your Gut. Your body knows when it's overpowered. Don't let ego or session pressure override your instinct. The best session is one where you're in control and having fun, not one where you gritted your teeth through danger.

Not Considering Kite Type. A wave kite is more forgiving in heavy wind than a freeride or freestyle kite. If you're on a freestyle kite and struggling, a smaller size will help. If you're on a wave kite, you may have more tolerance.

Surf Store Recommendation

Building a quiver that covers all conditions is the real answer. We stock kites from Duotone, Cabrinha, and NeilPryde in a range of sizes--typically 9m, 12m, 14m, 17m, and 19m. For most European riders, a 14m and 17m combination covers 80% of conditions. Beginners and lighter riders often benefit from starting with 12m and 14m to build confidence before sizing up.

At Surf Store, our staff have real on-water experience and can help you choose the right progression path. Visit surf-store.com to browse our kite selection and talk through which sizes match your weight, skill level, and local wind patterns. Smart kite choices are the foundation of safe, progressive sessions.

title: When Should You Fly a Bigger Kite? — Underpowered Guide
meta_title: When to Fly a Bigger Kite | Light Wind Guide 2026
meta_description: Learn when to fly a bigger kite in light winds and avoid underpowered sessions. Expert tips for choosing the right size. Shop kites at Surf Store.
excerpt: Fly a bigger kite when the wind drops below your current setup's minimum, typically in light winds under 10 knots where you feel underpowered and struggle to...
body_html:
You should fly a bigger kite when you feel underpowered in light wind conditions, usually below 12 knots, and struggling to maintain board speed or upwind performance. A larger kite generates more power from the same wind, letting you stay out longer and actually enjoy yourself rather than fighting to stay upright. The key is recognising when your current kite has stopped responding properly to your input.

The Full Answer

Light wind kitesurfing is one of the most rewarding challenges in the sport, but it demands the right equipment. When conditions drop below your current kite's sweet spot, you're fighting an uphill battle. A bigger kite isn't just about having more area--it's about matching your equipment to the actual wind available.

Most riders carrying a single kite often find themselves either overpowered in moderate winds or severely underpowered when the breeze drops. If you're constantly depowering, struggling to get going from a standstill, or finding yourself drifting downwind instead of progressing upwind, you're underpowered. This is when a bigger kite becomes essential.

The transition point depends on your weight, skill level, and local wind patterns. Lighter riders may need to switch to a larger setup at 13-14 knots, while heavier riders might operate comfortably until 10-11 knots. Your board choice matters too--smaller, lighter boards need less power, so you might not need to go as large.

Practical Guide

Step 1: Recognise Underpowering Signs

  • You can't maintain speed on a reach or upwind course
  • You're fully powered (bar fully in) but still feeling sluggish
  • You need help to launch, or the kite struggles to generate lift
  • You're spending most of your session drifting or paddling back

Step 2: Check Wind Conditions Properly

Don't rely on weather station readings alone--they're often recorded at height and inland. Look at the water texture: ripples mean light wind, small wavelets mean moderate wind. If you're seeing mostly flat water with occasional small waves, you're likely in the 8-12 knot range where a bigger kite helps enormously.

Step 3: Choose Your Bigger Kite Size

As a rule of thumb, jump up one size category--from a 15m to a 17m, or from a 14m to a 16m. If you're already at your kite's maximum (17m), you'll need to switch brands or styles; some designs are more efficient in light wind than others. Directional or wave kites tend to perform better when underpowered than freestyle kites because they're designed for control in varied conditions.

Step 4: Adjust Your Technique

A bigger kite requires slightly different handling. Keep smoother bar movements, use more kite height to generate power, and don't over-sheet--light wind kites work best with a responsive touch. Your stance matters: stay centred over the board and use your body weight effectively.

Common Mistakes

Waiting Too Long to Upsize

Many riders soldier on with an undersized kite, convincing themselves they'll manage. This doesn't develop skill--it just frustrates you. If the wind is genuinely light, accept it and grab the bigger kite. You'll actually have more fun and progress faster because you're riding with power rather than struggling.

Jumping Up Too Much

Going from 15m straight to 19m when wind drops is overkill unless conditions are genuinely extreme. You'll overshoot the sweet spot and be difficult to control. Upsize by 1-2m increments, especially when learning.

Ignoring Board-to-Kite Balance

A bigger kite needs a board that can handle the extra power. If you're using a very small freestyle board with a 19m kite, you'll struggle with lift-off and control. Make sure your board matches the kite's power delivery.

Not Respecting Lighter Wind Handling

Light wind kites are twitchier and respond more to edge and pressure changes. If you treat a 17m in 10 knots like you'd treat it in 14 knots, you'll overpower the kite and stall it. Use gentler bar movements and trust the kite's design.

At Surf Store, we stock kites from Duotone, Cabrinha, NeilPryde, and Gaastra--all excellent for light wind work. Duotone and Cabrinha offer ranges specifically designed for underpowered conditions, with responsive feel and efficient power generation. If you're expanding your quiver, a 17m directional or all-round kite pairs beautifully with our selection of smaller wave and freestyle boards from JP Australia, Fanatic, and Nobile.

Our team has real on-water experience across European wind patterns. If you're unsure about sizing, reach out or visit us--we'll help you choose the right bigger kite for your weight, skill level, and local conditions. Browse our complete kite range at surf-store.com, or contact us for personalised advice on stepping up to bigger kites for light wind.

title: Wetsuit Thickness by Water Temperature — Full Guide 2026
meta_title: Wetsuit Thickness Water Temperature Guide 2026
meta_description: Match wetsuit thickness to water temperature with our practical guide chart. Learn when to wear 3/2mm, 4/3mm, 5/4mm suits. Shop now at Surf Store.
excerpt: Choose wetsuit thickness by matching millimetre rating to your water temperature: 3/2mm for 16–18°C, 4/3mm for 12–16°C, 5/4mm for 8–12°C, 6/5mm for below 8°C.
body_html:
The right wetsuit thickness keeps you warm, comfortable, and focused on your session instead of shivering. Your choice depends on water temperature: use 3-4mm for cold water (below 10°C), 2-3mm for cool water (10-16°C), and 1-2mm for warm water (above 16°C). Beyond temperature, your own cold tolerance, session length, and activity intensity all matter--so we'll walk you through the full logic.

The Full Answer

Wetsuit thickness is measured in millimetres and refers to the neoprene layer that traps a thin film of water against your skin. Your body warms that water, creating an insulating layer. The thicker the neoprene, the more insulation it provides--but also the less flexible it is.

Water temperature is the primary driver of your choice, but it is not the only one. A wetsuit thickness water temperature guide gives you a starting point, but real-world conditions vary:

  • Cold water (below 10°C): 4-5mm suits, often with a hood and gloves. Essential in winter across northern Europe.
  • Cool water (10-16°C): 3-4mm full suits. The most common range for UK, Scandinavia, and Atlantic-facing coasts in autumn and spring.
  • Mild water (16-20°C): 2-3mm full suits or spring suits (long arms, short legs).
  • Warm water (above 20°C): 1-2mm suits or rashguards. Summer in the Mediterranean and southern Europe.

Your individual cold tolerance matters too. Some people run warm; others feel cold even in mild conditions. If you have done plenty of sessions, you know your own bias--factor that in. Session length also counts: a 45-minute blast needs less insulation than a 3-hour swell day.

Practical Guide

Step 1: Check your local water temperature. Look at regional surf forecasts or coastal water-temperature data for your spot. European water temperatures swing dramatically: the North Sea peaks at 15-17°C in August and drops to 4-6°C in January. The Mediterranean stays warmer--15-18°C year-round for most coasts.

Step 2: Identify your activity and session length. Kitesurfing and wing foiling sessions often last 1-2 hours. Windsurfing and SUP can stretch to 3+ hours. Longer sessions demand thicker suits.

Step 3: Choose your suit type. A full suit (4/3 or 3/2) covers arms and legs. A spring suit covers the torso and long arms but has short legs--ideal for shoulder seasons. A rashguard or thin 1mm suit works for summer. The first number (e.g. 4/3) is chest/torso thickness; the second is arm and leg thickness.

Step 4: Add extras if needed. In very cold water, a hood, gloves, and booties are not optional--they prevent heat loss at the extremities. Most European winter riders pair a 4/3 suit with these accessories.

Step 5: Test and adjust. If you shiver during your first 10 minutes, you need more thickness. If you feel overheated after 30 minutes, you may be over-suited. A good fit--snug but not restrictive--is as important as thickness.

Common Mistakes

  • Ignoring air temperature. A 15°C water day is very different in August (air 25°C) versus December (air 5°C). In winter, you get cold getting in and out of the water, not just in it.
  • Choosing by brand or look instead of thickness. A beautiful suit is no good if it does not fit the conditions. Always prioritise the number (4mm, 3mm, etc.) first.
  • Buying one suit for all seasons. If you ride year-round in northern Europe, you need at least two: a 4/3 or 5/4 for winter, and a 2/3 spring suit or 2mm full suit for summer.
  • Neglecting fit. A suit that is too loose lets water flush in and out, defeating the insulation. Too tight, and you restrict movement and blood flow. Measure your chest, waist, and height--most brands size by these.
  • Forgetting accessories in very cold water. No suit keeps your fingers, toes, and head warm on their own. A 4/3 without gloves and a hood is incomplete in 5°C water.

Surf Store Recommendation

At Surf Store, we stock premium wetsuits from ION and Gaastra--two brands trusted by European water sports pros. ION specialises in flexible, durable neoprene with excellent stretch and recovery, making them ideal for windsurfing and wing foiling where freedom of movement counts. Gaastra suits offer solid thermal protection and a refined fit across the range.

We carry full suits in 5/4, 4/3, 3/2, and 2/3 thicknesses, plus spring suits and rashguards for warmer months. Our team can help you pick the right thickness and fit for your location and session style. Visit surf-store.com to browse current stock and ask our staff--we have tested these suits on the water ourselves.

title: Kite Size by Weight & Wind Speed — Full Calculator Guide
meta_title: Kite Size Calculator by Weight & Wind Speed 2026
meta_description: Learn to choose the right kite size using our weight and wind speed chart. Expert guidance for kitesurfers. Shop now at Surf Store.
excerpt: Match your kite size to your body weight and local wind conditions using a simple chart and three-step formula for safe, responsive riding.
body_html:
Your ideal kite size is determined by three factors: your body weight, the wind speed at your location, and your riding style. Heavier riders and lighter wind conditions call for bigger kites; lighter riders and stronger wind favour smaller kites. A practical rule of thumb is that most riders between 60-80 kg need a 14-17 m² kite in moderate wind (12-18 knots), but this changes dramatically with conditions and experience.

The Full Answer

Choosing kite size by weight and wind speed isn't guesswork--it's physics. Your kite generates lift and power based on its surface area and the wind's energy. A heavier rider needs more power to get going; a stronger wind pushes you harder, so you need less area to stay in control.

Professional kitesurfers use a kite size calculator weight wind speed chart to match three variables:

  • Body weight: Ranges from 50 kg to 100+ kg. Lighter riders can use smaller kites in the same wind.
  • Wind speed: Measured in knots (nautical miles per hour). Most kite spots average 10-25 knots depending on season and location.
  • Riding style: Freestyle and wave riders prefer smaller, more responsive kites. Cruising and racing riders favour larger kites for efficiency.

As a rule, you'll typically own 2-3 kites to cover your local wind range. A 17 m² works in light wind; a 14 m² in moderate; a 12 m² in strong wind.

Practical Guide

Step 1: Know your weight range. Are you 50-60 kg, 60-80 kg, 80-100 kg, or over 100 kg? This is your baseline.

Step 2: Check your local average wind. If your spot typically runs 12-18 knots, you're in moderate wind. Light wind is under 12 knots; strong wind is 18+ knots.

Step 3: Use this simple kite size chart:

Rider Weight Light Wind (8-12 knots) Moderate Wind (12-18 knots) Strong Wind (18+ knots)
50-60 kg 17-19 m² 14-16 m² 10-12 m²
60-80 kg 17-19 m² 14-17 m² 12-14 m²
80-100 kg 19-21 m² 17-19 m² 14-17 m²
100+ kg 21+ m² 19-21 m² 17-19 m²

Step 4: Think about control. Beginners and lighter wind riders should stay on the larger side. Experienced riders can push smaller sizes for responsiveness.

Step 5: Test before you buy. If possible, rent or borrow different sizes. A 14 m² from Duotone feels different from a 14 m² Cabrinha--brand, shape, and design matter.

Common Mistakes

Mistake 1: Ignoring wind direction and gusts. Onshore wind is more forgiving but less powerful. Offshore wind packs a punch. Gusty conditions favour slightly smaller kites.

Mistake 2: Buying only one kite. This is the biggest trap. You'll quickly outgrow a single size or face days when wind is too light or too strong.

Mistake 3: Confusing kite size with kite type. A 17 m² freestyle kite handles differently from a 17 m² wave kite. Know what you want to do first.

Mistake 4: Overestimating wind speed. Most beginners think the wind is stronger than it is. Use an anemometer or check local wind reports--don't guess.

Mistake 5: Forgetting about skill level. A beginner at 75 kg needs a more forgiving, bigger kite than an expert at the same weight.

Surf Store Recommendation

At Surf Store, we stock quality kites from Duotone, Cabrinha, NeilPryde, and Gaastra across the full range of sizes--from 10 m² up to 21 m²--so you can build a quiver that matches your weight and your local wind. Our team has real on-water experience and can help you choose by describing how each brand feels in light, moderate, and strong conditions. We also recommend starting with two sizes: a mid-range kite (14-17 m²) for your average wind, and either a larger one for light days or a smaller one for strong wind. Contact us at Surf Store or visit our shop to try before you buy--we're here to help you get the right kite for your conditions.

title: Windsurf Sail Size per Wind Conditions — How to Choose
meta_title: Windsurf Sail Size Guide by Wind Conditions 2026
meta_description: Learn how to choose the right windsurf sail size for your wind conditions. Expert guide with practical tips. Shop now at Surf Store.
excerpt: Match your windsurf sail size to wind speed and your weight: lighter winds need larger sails (5.0–6.5 m²), medium winds suit 4.5–5.5 m², and strong winds req...
body_html:
The right windsurf sail size depends on wind strength, your weight, and your skill level. In lighter winds (8-12 knots), you'll want a larger sail like a 5.5-6.5 m² to generate power; in strong winds (18+ knots), a smaller sail like 3.5-4.5 m² keeps you in control. Getting this match right transforms your sessions from frustrating to flowing.

The Full Answer

Choosing the correct windsurf sail size per wind conditions guide is one of the most important skills you'll develop. It's not just about comfort--it's about safety, learning speed, and enjoyment on the water.

Wind strength is measured in knots, and every sail has a recommended wind range printed on its battens. A 5.0 m² sail might be rated for 12-18 knots, while a 7.0 m² is best in 8-14 knots. These ranges are starting points, not absolutes.

Your weight matters. A heavier sailor (90+ kg) will need a larger sail in the same wind than a lighter sailor (60-70 kg) because they need more lift to get going. Conversely, a light sailor on a 5.5 m² in 14 knots will have plenty of control, while the same sail on a heavier sailor in those conditions might feel underpowered.

Skill level is critical. Beginners should favour sails that are slightly oversized for conditions--easier to uphaul, easier to control, more forgiving. Intermediate and advanced sailors can match sails more precisely to wind, or even go slightly underpowered to work on technique.

Practical Guide

Step 1: Know the wind forecast. Check your local forecast for sustained wind speed and gusts. Windsurf in 8-20 knots for most recreational sailing.

Step 2: Estimate your range. Use this as a starting point:

  • Light winds (8-12 knots): 6.0-7.5 m² sails
  • Moderate winds (12-16 knots): 4.5-5.5 m² sails
  • Strong winds (16-22 knots): 3.5-4.5 m² sails
  • Very strong winds (22+ knots): 2.5-3.5 m² sails (wave/freestyle)

Step 3: Adjust for your weight. If you're heavier than average, go 0.5 m² larger. If lighter, go 0.5 m² smaller.

Step 4: Test and adjust. On the water, you should be able to uphaul and jibe smoothly. If the sail feels sluggish to uphaul or you're using maximum effort, go larger next time. If you're constantly blown out or fighting control, go smaller.

Common Mistakes

Going too small too early. Beginners often underestimate and choose sails that are too small. This makes uphauling hard, learning progress slow, and frustration high. Start generous; you can always downsize.

Ignoring gust range. Wind rarely stays steady. If the forecast shows 10-18 knots, you need a sail that works across that range. A 5.0 m² is safer than a 6.0 m² because the gusts won't overwhelm you.

Using the same sail all season. One sail does not fit all conditions. Invest in at least 2-3 sails: one for light winds (6.0-7.0 m²), one for moderate (4.5-5.5 m²), and one for strong (3.5-4.0 m²).

Forgetting about water temperature. Thicker wetsuits add weight and bulk. In winter, account for this when choosing sail size.

Surf Store Recommendation

We stock quality windsurf sails from Duotone, NeilPryde, and Gaastra in sizes from 2.5 m² to 8.5 m², so you can build a quiver that covers your local wind range. Whether you need a large wave sail, a freestyle slalom sail, or a freeride all-rounder, our expert team can match the right brand and size to your weight and skill level. Stop in or get in touch before buying--we'll ask about your typical wind, your weight, and what you want to improve, and we'll point you to the best options.

Final Thought

Windsurf sail size per wind conditions is a skill, not a science. Keep a logbook: note the wind speed, your sail choice, and how it felt. Over a few sessions, you'll develop intuition and confidence. Start at Surf Store with a consultation--we're here to help you dial it in.

title: Wing Foil Size Guide — Choosing Your Wing by Wind Speed
meta_title: Wing Foil Size Guide by Wind Speed 2026
meta_description: Learn how to choose the right wing foil size for your wind conditions. Beginner to advanced tips. Find it at Surf Store.
excerpt: Match your wing foil size to wind speed: lighter winds need bigger wings (5–6 m²), medium winds 4–5 m², and strong winds 3–4 m².
body_html:
The golden rule is simple: lighter wind demands larger wing foil size, stronger wind demands smaller size. A beginner in 10 knots needs a bigger wing than an advanced rider in 18 knots--your wing foil size by wind speed determines whether you have enough power to get going or stay in control.

The Full Answer

Wing foil size by wind speed is determined by how much power you need to generate lift and forward motion. Lighter winds (8-14 knots) require larger wing foil sizes--typically 4.5-6.0 square metres--because a bigger wing catches more air and creates more drive. Moderate winds (14-20 knots) suit mid-range wing foil sizes of 3.5-5.0 square metres. Strong winds (20+ knots) call for smaller wing foil sizes of 2.5-4.0 square metres to prevent overpowering and maintain control.

Your personal skill level also matters. A beginner learning the basics will want to stay powered-up and forgiving, so they'll size up slightly compared to an advanced rider. An advanced or heavier rider (80+ kg) might need a larger wing in any given wind speed than a lighter or newer sailor. Wind consistency and gusts also play a role--if your forecast shows 12-16 knots with big swings, you might choose a slightly larger wing foil size by wind speed to stay comfortable in the lulls.

Most wing foilers own two or three wing sizes to cover their local wind range. A practical split for a European venue is a 5.0m for light days, a 4.0m for medium wind, and a 3.0m for strong wind or advanced sessions.

Practical Guide

Light Wind (8-14 knots)

  • Recommended wing foil size: 5.0-6.0 square metres
  • Best for: beginners learning to launch and glide, lighter riders, choppy or gusty conditions
  • Why: a large wing catches weak air and generates enough lift to get airborne and cruising
  • Example: a 70 kg beginner in 11 knots should start with a 5.5m wing foil

Moderate Wind (14-20 knots)

  • Recommended wing foil size: 3.5-5.0 square metres
  • Best for: intermediate to advanced riders, most recreational sessions, good wind days
  • Why: medium wing foil size by wind speed offers power, control, and room to progress tricks
  • Example: an 75 kg intermediate rider in 16 knots should use a 4.0-4.5m wing

Strong Wind (20+ knots)

  • Recommended wing foil size: 2.5-4.0 square metres
  • Best for: advanced riders, heavier sailors, freestyle and racing, consistency
  • Why: smaller wing foil stays responsive and prevents backflip scenarios when overpowered
  • Example: an 80 kg advanced rider in 24 knots should drop to a 2.5-3.0m wing

Common Mistakes

  • Choosing one wing and staying with it. A single wing foil size by wind speed rarely covers your whole range. You'll feel under-powered in light wind and over-powered in gusts. Plan to own at least two wing sizes.
  • Ignoring your weight. Heavier riders (85+ kg) need larger wings in the same wind. Lighter riders (60 kg or under) can go smaller. Wing foil size by wind speed is personal--don't copy your friend's setup if you're a different shape.
  • Confusing wing area with board size. A large wing and small board can work in strong wind; a small wing and large board work in light wind. Choose wing foil size by wind speed first, then match your board to the board sport (racing, freestyle, cruising).
  • Not accounting for gust range. If the forecast is 10-16 knots, pick the wing foil size by wind speed that suits 12-14 knots, not the absolute maximum. You can always pump harder in lulls.

Surf Store Recommendation

We stock wing foils from Duotone, Cabrinha, and NeilPryde in the full range of sizes--from 2.5m to 6.0m--to suit beginners through advanced riders across all European wind conditions. Rather than buying one wing and hoping, we recommend starting with a 4.5-5.0m wing foil if you're new, then adding a 3.5m wing once you're comfortable. If you're heavier or in a very light-wind location, begin with a 5.5m.

Our staff have years of on-water experience and can advise you on wing foil size by wind speed for your local break, weight, and skill level. We'll help you build a quiver that covers your entire season without overspending. Visit Surf Store to browse wings, check current stock, and speak with our team about the right sizes for you.

title: Kite Foiling in Light Wind — Minimum Wind & Technique Guide
meta_title: Kite Foiling Light Wind Guide 2026 | Surf Store
meta_description: Master kite foiling light wind techniques with the right gear. Learn minimum speed, board selection & safety tips. Shop now at Surf Store.
excerpt: Kite foiling light wind sessions demand the right combination of large kites, lightweight foil boards, and precise body positioning to generate lift and main...
body_html:
Understanding These Conditions

Light wind kite foiling is one of the most rewarding skills to master because it expands your riding window dramatically. When we talk about light wind kite foiling, we're typically looking at conditions between 8-12 knots--below the threshold where most traditional twintips become effortless, but absolutely rideable on a foil board with the right approach.

In these conditions, you're working with minimal apparent wind. Your foil board sits higher in the water than a twintip would, meaning less drag, less weight pressing down, and more responsiveness to every gust and adjustment you make. There's rarely significant swell in light wind--you're riding thermal or local wind patterns, often with choppy, inconsistent water conditions created by the wind itself rather than distant storms.

This scenario is common across European spots during summer thermal cycles, early mornings on the Mediterranean, or during transition wind periods. The water stays relatively flat, but that's actually an advantage: you can focus entirely on technique and foil control without worrying about navigating swell.

Best Gear for These Conditions

Kite foiling in light wind demands careful equipment selection because every gram and every square centimetre of kite area matters.

Kite Size

You'll want to go larger than you might for powered wind. A 15-17m kite is standard for light wind kite foiling; some riders push to 19m in truly marginal 8-knot conditions. Duotone and Cabrinha both make excellent light-wind foils kites with excellent drift and drift characteristics. NeilPryde kites also excel in these conditions, offering predictable power delivery across the window.

Foil Board & Size

This is where kite foiling in light wind truly shines. Choose a dedicated foil board in the 155-200cm range, depending on your weight and skill. Lighter riders (under 75kg) benefit from boards around 160-175cm; heavier riders need 180-200cm to generate enough lift for early takeoff. JP Australia and Fanatic both produce responsive foil boards designed for light-wind efficiency. Avoid twintips here--foil boards are purpose-built for this exact scenario.

Foil Setup

Larger wing foils (1600-1800cm²) are essential for light wind kite foiling. A smaller fuselage and thinner mast also reduce drag. Most foil boards come with adjustable setups; ensure your wing and stabiliser combination prioritises lift generation over speed.

Wetsuit

Light wind sessions often occur during warm months, so a 3/2mm or 2/2mm wetsuit from ION or NeilPryde is typically sufficient. However, if you're riding in spring or autumn light wind, a 4/3mm wetsuit provides better protection without bulk.

Technique Tips

Light wind kite foiling demands smooth, deliberate movements--jerky actions waste the little energy you have.

  • Stay centred and patient: Position yourself in the middle of your board with slightly forward weight bias. Wait for wind gusts rather than fighting flat spells. Foil boards are forgiving; trust the board to find the water once you're moving.
  • Use the entire kite window: In light wind, work your kite across the full power zone (roughly 45° either side of zenith) to maintain steady tension. Small, flowing figure-eight motions keep energy in the system without violent yanking.
  • Body positioning: Keep your body relaxed and upright. Excessive heel or toe pressure buries the rail unnecessarily. Let the foil do the work; your role is to stay stable and guide with subtle weight shifts.
  • Early foiling: Foils unlock water-start capabilities in marginal wind. Once you're moving, let the board climb progressively--don't force it. A smooth, early liftoff is better than aggressive pumping that breaks flow.
  • Gybing and transitions: In light wind, transitions are smoother because you're moving slower. Practise wide, flowing gybes with continuous bar pressure. Avoid sudden movements that stall the kite.

Safety Checklist

  • Land positioning: Always keep clear of the kite landing zone. Light wind can turn gusty without warning, and a large 15-17m kite falling uncontrolled is dangerous. Use a buddy or secured landing area.
  • Equipment inspection: Check your foil (mast, wing, and fuselage) for hairline cracks before every session. Light wind rides are long, and a compromised foil can fail unexpectedly under load.
  • Relaunch confidence: Relaunching a foil board in light wind takes practice. Ensure you're comfortable with self-rescue and board recovery before venturing far from shore.
  • Kite bar control: A light wind session means extended hang time. Stay focused on bar feel and hand position--fatigue is your enemy. Take breaks before your grip weakens.
  • Water temperature: Light wind often means longer sessions in cooler water. Dress appropriately to avoid hypothermia, especially in spring and autumn.

Pair these with a 15-17m kite from Duotone or Cabrinha--both brands excel at delivering smooth, predictable power in marginal conditions.
